1.description: Content Management Systems (CMS) let non-developers create, edit, and publish website content through an administrative dashboard. They power a large share of the public web (WordPress alone serves an estimated 40%+ of sites) and typically combine server-rendered HTML with extensible plugin or theme ecosystems that can inject client-side trackers.
1.name: CMS
1.summary_privacy: CMS sites frequently ship default analytics, social embeds, comment systems, and marketing plugins that drop third-party cookies and beacons before consent. Admin areas process editor PII and logged-in user data (forms, accounts, comments). Review the rendered tag list, cookie banner behavior, embedded fonts/CDNs, GDPR/CCPA disclosures, data-processor agreements, and whether the CMS hosts EU data in-region.
1.summary_security: Treat any exposed CMS as a high-value target: ~90% of WordPress CVEs come from plugins/themes, with XSS, CSRF, SQLi, and arbitrary file upload dominating. Check for outdated cores (Drupalgeddon-class RCE), unauthenticated admin endpoints, weak/default admin credentials, exposed /wp-admin or /user/login, XML-RPC abuse, and writable upload directories. Verify auto-update, WAF, MFA on admin, and that backup/staging copies are not publicly indexed.

2.description: Message board / forum software hosts public or semi-public threaded discussions, user accounts, private messages, and avatars. Typical examples include phpBB, vBulletin, XenForo, MyBB, Discourse, and Simple Machines Forum. Forums create persistent user-generated content stores and are a magnet for spam, credential stuffing, and account-takeover campaigns.
2.name: Message boards
2.summary_privacy: Forums store user email addresses, IPs, password hashes, signatures, private messages, and often integrate Gravatar, OAuth, embedded YouTube/Twitter, or Disqus, all of which leak data to third parties. Public profiles can expose PII. Confirm GDPR/CCPA data-export and erasure flows, IP-logging retention, cookie-consent for embedded media, scrubbing of EXIF on uploads, and whether DMs are encrypted at rest. Forum DB dumps appear regularly on breach-trading sites.
2.summary_security: Forums are historically high-CVE software (vBulletin CVE-2025-48827/48828 unauth RCE, XenForo CVE-2024-38457/38458, recurring phpBB ACP issues). Check version disclosure, outdated installs, unmaintained mods/plugins, SSRF in URL previews, BBCode/Markdown XSS, file-upload RCE, weak avatar handling, and exposed admin panels. Verify MFA on staff accounts, CAPTCHA on registration, rate-limited login, password hashing strength, and patch latency.

3.description: Database managers are web-based admin UIs (phpMyAdmin, Adminer, phpPgAdmin, SQL Buddy) used to inspect and modify the underlying database directly through a browser. They are operational tooling, normally meant for staff or developers, but are often accidentally left reachable from the public internet alongside the application.
3.name: Database managers
3.summary_privacy: A database manager exposes the entire row-level data store, meaning every privacy regime that touches the underlying data (GDPR, CCPA, HIPAA, PCI-DSS for cardholder tables) is in scope. Unauthorized access here is a notifiable breach in most jurisdictions. Audit logging is usually absent or minimal, so exfiltration may be invisible. Should never be public; if discovered, treat as potential PII/PHI/PCI breach until proven otherwise.
3.summary_security: Finding a database manager fingerprinted on a public site is a critical red flag. History: phpMyAdmin CVE-2018-12613 (LFI->RCE), recurring SQLi/XSS/CSRF (CVE-2022-0813), Adminer SSRF/arbitrary-file-read (CVE-2021-21311, pre-4.7.9). Shodan/Censys routinely surface thousands of exposed instances under default paths (/phpmyadmin, /adminer.php). Check for default creds, missing IP allow-list, no MFA/SSO, outdated version, and whether the panel is reachable over plain HTTP.

4.description: Documentation tools generate product, API, or knowledge-base sites from source markdown / reStructuredText / OpenAPI specs. Common examples include MkDocs, Docusaurus, Sphinx, Read the Docs, GitBook, BookStack, and DocFX. The rendered output is usually mostly static, but the content frequently leaks internal architecture, endpoints, or even secrets.
4.name: Documentation
4.summary_privacy: Docs sites typically collect little user data themselves, but often embed search providers (Algolia DocSearch), analytics (GA, Plausible), and feedback widgets that drop cookies and beacons. GitBook, Read the Docs, and similar SaaS hosts process visitor IPs and may log queries. Check for consent banners on EU visitors, third-party search/CDN data flows, and whether reader accounts (where applicable) are tied to email/SSO.
4.summary_security: Static-rendered docs sites have small runtime attack surface, but watch for: secrets baked into client bundles (Docusaurus gists plugin CVE-2025-53624 leaked PATs), outdated React/webpack dependencies, MkDocs plugin path-traversal (CVE-2026-7159), and Jinja/PyYAML/Markdown transitive CVEs. Also check whether the docs reveal internal endpoints, staging URLs, API tokens, or admin paths. Self-hosted authoring backends (BookStack, GitBook on-prem) need normal app hardening.

5.description: Widgets are embeddable third-party components a site drops onto its pages: chat buttons, booking tools, social embeds, PDF viewers, review badges, audio players, contact forms, and similar. They are typically loaded as remote JavaScript or iframes from a vendor's CDN and run with the same DOM/cookie/form access as first-party code.
5.name: Widgets
5.summary_privacy: Widgets routinely read form fields, cookies, scroll/click events, and full DOM, then ship that data to the vendor's servers, often outside the EU. Most drop tracking cookies or local-storage IDs. Confirm consent-mode gating, that widgets defer load until consent is given, vendor's GDPR/CCPA posture (DPA, sub-processors, data residency), and disclosure in the privacy policy. Social embeds (YouTube, Twitter, Facebook) are notorious for setting third-party cookies pre-consent.
5.summary_security: Every widget is a supply-chain dependency: a compromised vendor (Polyfill.io 2024, Magecart-style script injection) instantly compromises every embedding site. Verify use of Subresource Integrity (SRI), a strict Content-Security-Policy that restricts script-src, and that widget scripts are loaded over HTTPS from pinned origins. Watch for widgets that dynamically load further scripts ("shadow scripts"). Iframe-isolated widgets are materially safer than inline JS widgets.

6.description: Ecommerce platforms run online stores: product catalogs, carts, checkout, customer accounts, orders, and (directly or via gateway) payment capture. Examples include Magento/Adobe Commerce, Shopify, WooCommerce, BigCommerce, PrestaShop, Salesforce Commerce Cloud, and many regional SaaS storefronts. They process cardholder data flows and large volumes of customer PII, putting them squarely in PCI-DSS and GDPR scope.
6.name: Ecommerce
6.summary_privacy: Stores hold names, addresses, phone numbers, order history, marketing consents, and often hashed passwords, with PCI-DSS strictly governing cardholder data. They typically embed many third-party tags (analytics, retargeting, A/B test, chat, reviews, fraud), each a data-flow to disclose. Check cookie banner, consent-mode, data residency for EU customers, retention of order PII, GDPR subject-access flows, child-purchase controls (COPPA where applicable), and breach-notification readiness.
6.summary_security: Ecommerce sites are the #1 e-skimming target; Magecart activity surged ~103% in 2024-25. Magento/Adobe Commerce CosmicSting (CVE-2024-34102), CVE-2024-20720, SessionReaper (CVE-2025-54236) drove mass compromise. Verify PCI-DSS 4.0 reqs 6.4.3/11.6.1 (script inventory, SRI, tamper detection on payment pages), strict CSP, patched core+extensions, WAF on /checkout, admin MFA, and tokenised card capture via gateway rather than store backend.

7.description: Photo gallery software displays, organises, and sometimes sells images on a website: lightboxes, sliders, image management apps, and photographer portfolio platforms. Examples include Piwigo, Coppermine, Lychee, NextGEN Gallery, PhotoSwipe, SmugMug, Slider Revolution, and various WordPress gallery plugins. Many allow user uploads and integrate with payment or licensing.
7.name: Photo galleries
7.summary_privacy: Image files routinely carry EXIF metadata (GPS, device serial, capture time, sometimes author/email). Confirm uploads are stripped server-side; otherwise downloads leak photographer or subject location. Portfolio SaaS galleries log viewer IPs; client-proofing collects buyer email/payment data. Children's photos invoke COPPA. Embedded lightboxes/CDNs may set cookies. Watch for indexable private albums and predictable share-URL patterns.
7.summary_security: Image-handling code has a long history of upload/path-traversal/SSRF bugs. Piwigo: SQLi (CVE-2026-27634), missing-authz (CVE-2026-27833), repeated XSS; Coppermine: file-upload RCE, path traversal (CVE-2026-3013); NextGEN Gallery had unauth file-upload RCE (2017). Slider Revolution famously enabled mass WordPress compromise. Check for outdated gallery plugins, file-upload validation, image-library CVEs (ImageMagick, libwebp), EXIF stripping, and reachable admin paths.

8.description: Wiki software powers collaboratively edited knowledge bases with versioned pages, permissions, and inline media. Examples include MediaWiki (which runs Wikipedia), Atlassian Confluence, DokuWiki, XWiki, TWiki/Foswiki, Tiki, and TiddlyWiki. They are common for internal documentation and public reference sites and tend to mix anonymous read access with authenticated editing.
8.name: Wikis
8.summary_privacy: Public wikis log every edit with username or IP, often shown in page history forever. Confirm anonymous-edit IP masking, GDPR erasure on edit history, retention policy, and that private wikis don't publish indexable pages. SaaS wikis (Confluence Cloud) process org PII in a vendor's region: review DPA and data residency. Internal wikis routinely accumulate secrets, credentials, and customer data; access controls and search scoping matter as much as cookies.
8.summary_security: Wikis are recurring high-severity CVE targets. Confluence CVE-2022-26134 (OGNL pre-auth RCE) and CVE-2023-22515 (privesc to admin) saw mass exploitation, cryptominers, and ransomware. MediaWiki ships 13-20 CVEs/year (Parsoid XSS, ext SSRF eg CVE-2025-32699); DokuWiki has repeated XSS in RSS/parser. Check version, exposed admin/API endpoints, extension patch level, anonymous-edit permissions, account-creation throttling, upload restrictions, SSO/MFA on admins.

9.description: Hosting panels are web-based administrative interfaces (cPanel, Plesk, DirectAdmin, Pterodactyl, etc.) that let site owners and providers manage servers, domains, databases, mail, files, and billing through a browser. They typically run server-side with privileged access to the underlying operating system and many tenant accounts. As consolidated control planes, a single compromise can cascade across every site or customer the panel manages.
9.name: Hosting panels
9.summary_privacy: Panels concentrate tenant PII: domain owner data, billing, customer email/databases, server logs, backup contents. Not user trackers themselves, but a breach exposes everything they administer including downstream visitor data. Some panels phone home for license/update checks; commercial SaaS variants (billing panels, game-server hosts) may send analytics/telemetry. Data residency depends on the host operator, not the panel; GDPR controllership sits with the hosting provider.
9.summary_security: High-value targets: a single auth bypass (e.g. cPanel CVE-2026-41940 CRLF/session injection, CVSS 9.8) or Plesk RCE (CVE-2025-66430) compromises every hosted tenant. Check for: public /cpanel, /whm, :2083, :8443 exposure; default/weak admin creds; outdated builds; missing 2FA; CSRF/XSS in subscription pages; SQL injection history; root-level privilege escalation; unpatched ImageMagick/PHP stacks. Restrict by IP, enable 2FA, monitor login logs.

10.description: Analytics technologies measure how visitors use a site: page views, sessions, events, conversions, user flows, A/B tests, and product usage. They typically inject a third-party JavaScript snippet that beacons data back to the vendor (Google Analytics, Adobe, Matomo, Plausible, Mixpanel, etc.). Beyond simple traffic counting, many also collect IPs, device fingerprints, referrers, and behavioural traces, which makes the category a primary privacy-law touchpoint.
10.name: Analytics
10.summary_privacy: Highest-impact privacy category: transmits user behaviour to third parties, sets persistent IDs, often canvas/device fingerprints. EU DPAs ruled Google Analytics non-compliant post-Schrems II - Austrian DSB (Jan 2022), CNIL (Feb 2022), Italian Garante (June 2022), Danish, Norwegian, Hungarian followed. Requires prior opt-in under GDPR/ePrivacy; CCPA opt-out. Check IP anonymisation, EU residency, signed DPA, consent-mode, retention, and reuse for the vendor's own ad graph (Google Signals).
10.summary_security: Client-side JS supply-chain risk: any compromised analytics tag becomes a Magecart vector (Polyfill.io 2024 hit 100k+ sites; axios npm RAT 2026). Check for unpinned third-party script tags without SRI, missing CSP, vendor domains loading sub-scripts, tag-manager containers that bypass review, custom event payloads leaking session tokens or order IDs in URLs. Beacons over GET leak referers and PII in query strings. GA tag injection via compromised GTM containers is a recurring pattern.

11.description: Blog technologies are publishing platforms and engines (WordPress, Ghost, Substack, Medium, Tumblr, Hatena Blog, Dotclear, Typecho, etc.) used to author and distribute articles, newsletters, and personal sites. They are typically server-rendered apps that store posts in a database, expose an admin UI, and accept reader interactions like comments and subscriptions. Because they range from self-hosted PHP to commercial SaaS newsletters, security and privacy posture varies enormously across instances.
11.name: Blogs
11.summary_privacy: Self-hosted blogs collect author/subscriber emails, IPs in access logs, and comment metadata; many embed analytics, social widgets, ads, and CDN fonts that act as trackers. Hosted SaaS (Substack, Medium) profile readers, send newsletter open-tracking pixels, and may set marketing cookies. Subscription forms touch PII and trigger GDPR/ePrivacy consent and CCPA disclosures. Check for unconsented tracking pixels in email, IP logging duration, and third-party embed loads on article pages.
11.summary_security: Long CVE tail dominated by plugin/theme ecosystems: unauth SQLi (CVE-2024-27956 WP Automatic CVSS 9.9, CVE-2025-9807 Events Calendar), stored XSS (CVE-2024-2194, CVE-2023-40000), Ghost unauth DB read (3.24-6.19) and CVE-2022-41654 auth bypass. Common findings: outdated core/plugin versions, exposed /wp-admin and /ghost without rate-limiting or 2FA, weak XML-RPC, file-upload bypasses leading to RCE/webshells, comment-spam and brute-force on login.

12.description: JavaScript frameworks and libraries (React, Vue, Angular, Ember, Svelte, Backbone, jQuery, Next.js, Nuxt, etc.) provide the structure used to build interactive web applications. They run primarily in the user's browser to render UI, manage state, and handle routing, with some now also executing server-side. They are the foundation of nearly all modern web apps but also drag in large dependency trees from npm, expanding the supply-chain surface significantly.
12.name: JavaScript frameworks
12.summary_privacy: Frameworks themselves do not transmit user data; privacy impact is indirect via wired-in analytics, marketing SDKs, widgets, or public CDNs (unpkg, jsDelivr, Cloudflare) that log IPs. SSR frameworks may set first-party session cookies. No inherent regulatory trigger, but the runtime is where consent banners, fingerprinting, and tracker tags actually execute, so framework choice shapes how easy compliant patterns are.
12.summary_security: Framework primitives mitigate but do not prevent XSS - dangerouslySetInnerHTML, v-html, [innerHTML], unsanitised SSR markup still inject. Recent: CVE-2024-6783 Vue prototype-pollution XSS, CVE-2025-55182 Next.js deserialisation RCE, Angular template injection. Dominant risk is npm supply-chain (axios 2026 RAT, Polyfill.io 2024, event-stream). Enforce lockfiles, SRI on CDN scripts, CSP with nonce, SCA tooling.

13.description: Issue trackers are platforms for capturing, triaging, and resolving bugs, feature requests, and support tickets (Jira, GitHub Issues, GitLab, Redmine, Trac, Phabricator, Zammad, Bugzilla, Canny, BugHerd, etc.). They also commonly include error- and uptime-monitoring tools that automatically file issues (Sentry, GlitchTip, Honeybadger, Stackify). Most run server-side as multi-tenant SaaS or self-hosted apps; some embed a client widget that captures screenshots, console logs, and visitor metadata.
13.name: Issue trackers
13.summary_privacy: Tickets accumulate PII: emails, names, addresses, screenshots with session cookies/JWTs, DOM form values, console logs with API tokens, IPs, free-text payment/health details. Error monitors auto-capture stack traces and request bodies that may include credentials unless scrubbed. Most SaaS vendors are US-based, raising GDPR transfer concerns; check DPA, data residency, retention, and PII-redaction settings.
13.summary_security: Jira/Confluence pattern: SnakeYAML CVE-2022-1471 (CVSS 9.8), Email Templates RCE, workflow-import RCE, Velocity SSTI to deserialisation RCE - public-facing instances are scanned constantly. Self-hosted Bugzilla, Redmine, Phabricator carry their own XSS/CSRF history. Widget-based trackers (BugHerd, Marker, Ruttl) inject JS site-wide so a vendor compromise becomes a supply-chain vector. Restrict to SSO, patch promptly, lock /admin.

14.description: Video players are JavaScript or HTML5 components and embedded SaaS widgets that play streaming or on-demand video (YouTube, Vimeo, JW Player, Brightcove, Kaltura, Wistia, Video.js, Shaka Player, hls.js, Plyr, etc.). Most run as client-side iframes or scripts that pull manifests, segments, ads, and analytics from the vendor's edge network. Some are pure libraries; many ad-supported players (Aniview, STN Video) are tracker-heavy by design.
14.name: Video players
14.summary_privacy: YouTube iframes set VISITOR_INFO1_LIVE, YSC, PREF cookies and send IP, UA, referer to Google on first byte; even thumbnails transmit PII pre-consent under GDPR. youtube-nocookie / Vimeo DNT only partially mitigate. Commercial players (Brightcove, JW, Kaltura) ship view analytics, heatmaps, ad personalisation. COPPA applies for minors. Require consent before iframe load, prefer click-to-play placeholders.
14.summary_security: Library XSS history: Video.js CVE-2021-23414 (track-tag src bypass), CVE-2022-3985 / CVE-2022-4786 / CVE-2023-6485 in WordPress player plugins, Flash legacy via Ruffle. Iframe embeds from third parties can bypass CSP if frame-src is permissive. Ad-tech-heavy players (Aniview, STN) carry SSAI/VPAID code with elevated abuse history. Audit playlist/manifest URL handling, DRM license-server origins, postMessage handlers, and CSP frame-src/script-src.

15.description: Comment systems let visitors post replies, threaded discussions, and reactions under articles, videos, or product pages. They include hosted SaaS widgets (Disqus, IntenseDebate, Vuukle, Cackle, Livefyre), privacy-focused alternatives (Commento, Isso, Cove, ReplyBox), and GitHub-backed ones (Giscus, utterances). Most embed a third-party JavaScript widget that loads comments, identity flows, moderation tools, and (for commercial ones) ad-tech via the vendor's domain.
15.name: Comment systems
15.summary_privacy: Disqus was fined by Norwegian DPA in 2021 for unconsented tracking and ad-partner sharing; it remains a cross-site behavioural tracker logging IPs, UA, referers, and ad-IDs across every host. Vuukle, Cackle, IntenseDebate behave similarly. Self-hosted (Commento, Isso, utterances/Giscus) avoid third-party tracking but still touch commenter email and IP. GDPR/ePrivacy require prior consent before widget load; CCPA opt-out; COPPA if comments target minors.
15.summary_security: Stored XSS dominates - HTML Comment Box bypass exposed ~2M sites; Disqus, Vuukle, Cackle have repeated injection findings via Markdown/BBCode renderers, image proxies, avatar URLs. Third-party widget = supply-chain risk: vendor JS compromise injects script into every host page. Spam, scraped emails, OAuth/magic-link account-enumeration common. Use iframe sandbox, CSP script-src vendor pin, SRI, moderation.

16.description: The Security category covers a wide mix of protective technologies: CAPTCHAs (reCAPTCHA, hCaptcha, MTCaptcha, Friendly Captcha), bot- and fraud-detection (DataDome, HUMAN/PerimeterX, Kasada, Confiant), WAFs and DDoS mitigation, passwordless/2FA (Hanko), client-side script monitoring (c/side, RapidSec, Blue Triangle), anti-spam plugins, vulnerability scanners, consent/compliance automation, and identity verification. Most run as mixed deployments - an in-browser SDK that beacons to a cloud back-end - so they reduce attacker risk but add a powerful third-party data flow.
16.name: Security
16.summary_privacy: Detection works by fingerprinting: reCAPTCHA collects IPs, mouse/keystroke timing, browser/device fingerprints, plugins, screenshots; CNIL fined Cityscoot EUR 125k for unconsented use. DataDome/HUMAN/Kasada gather 35+ behavioural signals per session. Data typically leaves the EU to US clouds, triggering Schrems II concerns. hCaptcha and Friendly Captcha collect less. Consent banners sit here and define how all other trackers fire.
16.summary_security: Security tools are themselves targets: WAF/CDN bypass research, CAPTCHA-solving services, and a privileged client-side script footprint (full DOM, often eval). A compromise of a script-monitoring or bot SDK becomes a sitewide injection vector. Verify SOC2/ISO, SBOM, script-pinning, key rotation, fail-open vs fail-closed defaults, and that rules cannot be disabled by attacker headers. Plugins (CleanTalk, SiteGuard) have their own CVE history.

17.description: Font scripts deliver custom typography and icon glyphs to web pages, typically via @font-face declarations, JavaScript loaders, or third-party CDNs (e.g., Google Fonts, Adobe Fonts, Font Awesome, Bunny Fonts). They render text and iconography but commonly reach out to external hosts, exposing visitor IP addresses and request metadata to the font provider. Self-hosted alternatives (Fontsource, Bunny Fonts) preserve the visual function while eliminating third-party data transfer.
17.name: Font scripts
17.summary_privacy: Each remote font fetch transmits visitor IP, User-Agent and Referer to the font host. LG Muenchen I (3 O 17493/20, Jan 2022) ruled embedding Google Fonts via fonts.googleapis.com without consent breaches GDPR Art. 6, awarding EUR 100 damages and triggering a German waves-of-claims industry; CNIL/DSK guidance is aligned. Typekit ties usage to Adobe accounts; MyFonts and Hoefler log usage analytics. Self-hosted woff2 or Bunny Fonts (no-log, EU) avoid the transfer and cookie-banner question.
17.summary_security: Low direct attack surface: static glyph/woff2 delivery dominates and CVE history is thin. JS loaders (Cufon, sIFR, Font Awesome JS, Typekit kit.js) execute in-page and would amplify XSS if the CDN/account were compromised - every embedding site is affected. Pin versions, set SRI hashes, scope via CSP script-src/style-src, prefer self-hosted woff2 over remote JS loaders, and treat icon-JS bundles (Lucide React, Font Awesome JS) like any other npm dependency in SCA scanning.

18.description: Web frameworks are the foundational libraries and runtimes that structure a site's request handling, routing, templating, ORM, session state and API surface (e.g., Ruby on Rails, Laravel, Django, Next.js, Phoenix, ASP.NET, Spring). They live primarily on the server (or in SSR/hybrid edge runtimes) and dictate how user input, authentication and data persistence are processed. Framework choice strongly shapes a site's exposure to OWASP Top 10 categories.
18.name: Web frameworks
18.summary_privacy: Frameworks rarely transmit data to third parties, but handle the highest-sensitivity flows: credentials, session cookies, form POSTs, server-side PII, payment fields. Default logging can persist tokens/PII; default error pages can leak stack traces and DB schema. GDPR Art. 32, PCI DSS 4.0 and HIPAA outcomes depend almost entirely on operator config (cookie flags, HSTS, log redaction, secret rotation, retention), not on the framework. Defaults matter: opt-ins age into liabilities.
18.summary_security: Frameworks are the locus of OWASP Top 10 risk: injection, broken access control, SSRF, insecure deserialization, mass-assignment, template injection. Mature stacks ship CSRF, parameterised ORMs and output escaping by default; unsafe patterns (raw SQL, eval'd templates, debug-mode in prod) and outdated versions drive the CVE stream - Spring4Shell (CVE-2022-22965), Struts (CVE-2017-5638), Laravel (CVE-2021-3129), Next.js (CVE-2025-29927). Patch cadence, SCA and SSDF are essential.

19.description: Miscellaneous is the catch-all bucket in technology-fingerprinting taxonomies (Wappalyzer-style) for tools and signatures that do not fit a more specific category - utility widgets, niche SaaS, one-off plugins, HTTP curiosities and small vendor products. Because membership is heterogeneous, no single risk profile applies; assessment must defer to each individual technology's own scoring.
19.name: Miscellaneous
19.summary_privacy: Many entries are third-party SaaS embeds (Gravatar email-hash avatars, member portals, address-verification, niche commerce widgets) that drop cookies, beacon visitors, or load further trackers; others are protocols/build tools handling no user data. Never infer privacy posture from this category alone - rely on per-Technology privacy_score, tracker_density, vendor DPA and EDPB SCC posture. Cross-check obscure entries for EOL/abandonment.
19.summary_security: Heterogeneous catch-all: SaaS embeds (DocuSign, ServiceNow, Splunk, Apigee, Klaviyo Hub, Gravatar), build tools (Webpack, Vite, Babel), deprecated client plugins, and protocols (HTTP/2, HTTP/3, WebRTC, WebSocket). Blast radius varies extremely. Treat each detection as a trigger for individual review rather than a category-level signal: the presence of an unusual or end-of-life entry here is itself the indicator (supply-chain, missing updates, abandonment).

20.description: Editors are authoring tools used to produce or edit web content - in-browser code/text editors (Monaco, CodeMirror, Ace, Blockly), block-based site editors (Gutenberg), legacy site builders (DreamWeaver, FrontPage, iWeb) and online builder SaaS (Unbounce, CodeSandbox, BannerBoo). Most ship as JavaScript libraries that load inside an admin or builder UI rather than for end users; they touch authoring sessions and the site's underlying source.
20.name: Editors
20.summary_privacy: OSS editor libraries (Monaco, CodeMirror, Ace, Blockly) generally do not phone home. SaaS authoring platforms (CodeSandbox, Tiptap Cloud, Unbounce, Powtoon, Genially, BannerBoo) collect telemetry, drafts and load analytics/marketing trackers - sample tracker_density averages light to moderate. Drafts may include customer PII or unpublished business strategy; verify whether vendor AI/co-pilot features ingest content into training pipelines (sub-processor disclosures, EDPB AI guidance).
20.summary_security: Editors operate on untrusted content inside privileged authoring contexts, so XSS-via-paste, prototype pollution and sandbox escape recur (Draft.js, Gutenberg blocks, CodeMirror via Drupal advisories). Where exposed to non-admin users (CMS comments, form builders) sanitization gaps become stored XSS. Online builders (CodeSandbox - phishing-kit hosting cases) and legacy desktop tools (FrontPage, Dreamweaver) compound risk via stale templates. Strict CSP and version pinning are required.

21.description: Learning Management Systems host courses, assessments, grades and student-teacher communications - examples include Moodle, Canvas, Open edX, Sakai, Ilias, Blackboard, plus creator-economy platforms (Teachable, Thinkific, Podia, LearnWorlds). They consolidate large quantities of student PII, learner progress, payment data and minor-protected records, making them high-value breach targets.
21.name: LMS
21.summary_privacy: LMS stores government-regulated minor data (FERPA in the US, GDPR in the EU; COPPA where under-13s are present; state laws like SOPIPA, SOPPA). Hosted SaaS LMS adds transfer questions and vendor-side analytics. Creator platforms (Thinkific, Teachable, Kiwify) ship light/moderate tracker density and marketing pixels. Schools should require DPAs, data residency, encryption at rest, breach SLAs (GDPR 72h) and disable optional analytics where lawful basis is missing.
21.summary_security: LMS platforms are large multi-tenant web apps with file upload, plugin ecosystems, SSO/SAML, and grade-modification surfaces - classic injection / broken-access-control territory. The May 2026 Instructure Canvas / ShinyHunters breach (~275M users, 8,800+ institutions, 3.65 TB exfiltrated) is the watershed. Plugin marketplaces (Moodle, LifterLMS, Tutor LMS) widen the supply-chain surface; admin takeover cascades to every learner. SSO drift and OAuth token leakage are recurring root causes.

22.description: Web servers terminate HTTP(S) connections and dispatch requests to application code - nginx, Apache HTTP Server, Microsoft IIS, LiteSpeed, Tengine, Jetty, Tomcat / JBoss, Caddy, Cowboy, and WSGI/ASGI runtimes (Uvicorn, Gunicorn, Hypercorn). They are the first listener for every request, so misconfigurations and known CVEs become the perimeter risk for everything behind them.
22.name: Web servers
22.summary_privacy: Web servers do not phone home or set cookies, but access logs retain client IP, User-Agent and Referer in cleartext - personal data under GDPR Recital 30. Misconfigured directory listings, debug endpoints, source-disclosure CVEs (CVE-2024-38475, CVE-2024-39884) and exposed /server-status pages can leak environment, credentials and routing internals. Log redaction (strip IP last octet, rotate <=90 days), Apache LogFormat / nginx log_format tuning and retention policy define the privacy posture.
22.summary_security: Web servers are recurring CVE targets: Apache (CVE-2024-38475 mod_rewrite, exploited in the wild; on CISA KEV), nginx (CVE-2025-23419 SSL session reuse, ingress-nginx CVE-2025-1974 unauth RCE), plus persistent IIS, LiteSpeed, Tomcat advisories. HTTP/2 and HTTP/3 parser bugs, request smuggling, banner exposure and outdated TLS configs remain the practical attack vectors. Patch cadence is the single most important control; ingress controllers belong on the same CVE pager as the kernel.

23.description: Caching technologies store rendered HTML, API responses, object data or full pages closer to the client to reduce origin load - Varnish, nginx caches, LiteSpeed Cache, WP Rocket, W3 Total Cache, WordPress Super Cache, Redis Object Cache, Oracle Web Cache, plus edge-cache SaaS (NitroPack, QUIC.cloud, Azure CDN, Google PageSpeed). They sit between users and origin servers, transforming response routing and cache-key behaviour.
23.name: Caching
23.summary_privacy: Caching layers can accidentally cache authenticated or per-user responses (Set-Cookie, session pages, PII JSON) and serve them to other visitors - a classic data-leak path. Edge-CDN SaaS (Cloudflare, Azure CDN, NitroPack, QUIC.cloud, Sitecore Edge) terminate TLS and process traffic in third-country regions, raising GDPR Chapter V transfer questions; vendor RUM/analytics cookies are not default but are commonly added. Cache keys must include auth state; never cache Set-Cookie responses.
23.summary_security: Dominant risks are web-cache poisoning and cache deception (PortSwigger Kettle): unkeyed headers and path confusion let attackers persistently inject malicious responses served to all subsequent visitors. Object stores (Redis, Memcached) are routinely exposed unauthenticated - ~60k Redis instances and CVE-2025-49844 (RediShell, RCE) enable code execution. WordPress cache plugins are repeat offenders: LiteSpeed Cache CVE-2024-28000 (4M+ sites, privesc), W3TC and WP Super Cache stored-XSS.

24.description: Rich text / WYSIWYG editors let users author HTML content in the browser - TinyMCE, CKEditor, Quill, Froala, Editor.js, Trix, Tiptap, Etherpad, Summernote, Draft.js, Ace, Monaco. They convert user keystrokes into structured HTML/Markdown and persist it to the application backend, where it is later rendered to other users. They sit on the trust boundary between untrusted user input and rendered DOM.
24.name: Rich text editors
24.summary_privacy: Most rich text editors are local-only JavaScript and do not transmit content to third parties; SaaS variants (CKEditor Cloud, Tiptap Cloud, TinyMCE Cloud, Froala remote) transmit drafts, AI features and image uploads to vendor backends. Documents commonly contain PII or confidential business content, so sub-processors, data residency and EDPB SCC posture must be checked. Etherpad-style real-time collab broadens exposure to every pad participant; AI assistants raise training-data questions.
24.summary_security: Signature risk is stored XSS: the editor round-trips HTML, so any sanitization gap becomes a stored-XSS vector against every user who later views that content. CVE history shows recurring high-severity issues across TinyMCE (CVE-2024-29881, CVE-2025-27144 paste XSS), CKEditor (CVE-2024-45613, Drupal advisories), Froala (repeated 0-days), Quill (CVE-2023-26149) and Etherpad (CVE-2021-34816 RCE, CVE-2021-34817 XSS). Defence: strict server-side HTML sanitization, strict CSP, prompt updates.

25.description: JavaScript graphics encompasses client-side libraries for charts, plots, data visualisation, animations, and interactive SVG/Canvas/WebGL rendering (e.g., D3, Chart.js, Highcharts, Three.js, Paper.js). These libraries run in the user's browser, transforming structured data into visual representations, and are typically bundled or loaded via CDN as third-party scripts.
25.name: JavaScript graphics
25.summary_privacy: Pure visualisation libraries rarely transmit data themselves, so inherent privacy risk is low-to-moderate. The exposure comes from the data fed into them: charts often render personal, financial, or health metrics that then sit in DOM/memory and may leak via screenshots, error telemetry, or session-replay tools. Some commercial chart SaaS (e.g., LocalFocus, Variance) phone home for analytics, so review network behaviour and data-processing terms before sending PII to hosted variants.
25.summary_security: Client-side libraries that render untrusted data into the DOM, SVG, or Canvas, making DOM-based XSS the dominant risk (e.g., historical Highcharts XSS pre-v9 in tooltips/labels). Most are loaded from public CDNs without SRI, exposing supply-chain gaps. Verify versions against CVE feeds, sanitise chart inputs, pin via subresource-integrity, lock script-src/object-src in CSP, and avoid passing user HTML into chart options.

26.description: Mobile frameworks covers toolkits used to build mobile web apps, hybrid apps, or cross-platform mobile applications detectable from a website (e.g., jQuery Mobile, Framework7, Onsen UI, Sencha Touch, Ionic/Cordova-related stacks, "convert site to app" services). They influence how a site renders on mobile and frequently wrap content in a WebView when packaged for app stores.
26.name: Mobile frameworks
26.summary_privacy: Hybrid wrappers and "site-to-app" SaaS (Mobify, starti.app, Napps) can access device sensors, contacts, location, push tokens, and unique mobile identifiers via plugins, far exceeding what a plain mobile website could collect. SaaS conversion tools often inject analytics or attribution SDKs and route traffic through vendor infrastructure. Audit requested permissions, disclose SDK presence to users, honour ATT/IDFA and GDPR/CCPA opt-outs, and avoid frameworks that ship default tracker plugins.
26.summary_security: Mobile frameworks that wrap web content in a WebView (Cordova/PhoneGap-lineage, Framework7, Onsen UI hybrid mode) inherit browser-class risks plus mobile bridge risks: DOM/UXSS, JavaScript-to-native bridge abuse, weak whitelist/CSP, and historical issues such as Cordova WebViews ignoring TLS errors in debuggable builds. Apply OWASP MASVS controls, restrict WebView origins, enable CSP, pin certificates via plugins, and keep both the framework and Android System WebView current.

27.description: Programming languages covers server-side and full-stack languages/runtimes detectable on a website (e.g., PHP, Python, Node.js, Ruby, Java, Go, Rust, TypeScript, Perl, Scala, Elixir) as well as markup/standards like GraphQL, WebAssembly, and XSLT. Detection usually comes from response headers, cookie names, error pages, or runtime fingerprints rather than user-visible content.
27.name: Programming languages
27.summary_privacy: A language fingerprint alone does not collect personal data, so inherent privacy and tracking risk is very low. Privacy exposure is entirely a function of what the application built on top of it does with PII, logging, and third-party libraries. Pay attention to ecosystem-specific telemetry defaults (e.g., Node.js diagnostics, .NET telemetry, Flutter/Dart crash reporting) and to legacy stacks like Flash that historically exfiltrated more aggressively.
27.summary_security: The language syntax is not the threat; the runtime, stdlib, and ecosystem are. Memory-unsafe languages (C, C++, legacy PHP/Perl native extensions, Flash) drive ~70% of severe CVEs per CISA/NSA and Microsoft data, while managed languages (Go, Rust, Java, C#, Node/TS, Python) shift risk to injection, deserialisation, and dependency supply chain. Suppress X-Powered-By/Server banners, patch runtimes promptly, and track language-specific CVE feeds and EOL dates.

28.description: Operating systems identifies the underlying server or device OS reachable behind a website (Linux distributions such as Debian/Ubuntu/RHEL/CentOS/Alpine, Windows Server, FreeBSD, Darwin, embedded systems such as Hirschmann HiOS, UniFi OS, Windows CE). It is inferred from banner strings, response headers, TLS/SSH fingerprints, or HTTP errors.
28.name: Operating systems
28.summary_privacy: Operating systems themselves do not inherently collect end-user PII from website visitors, but they hold every log, secret, and cached personal record the site processes. A compromised or unpatched OS therefore becomes the largest PII-exposure event a site can suffer. Some vendor OSes phone home for telemetry (Windows Server, certain commercial Linux), so review opt-outs and ensure logs containing PII are access-controlled, retained per regulation, and not shipped to untrusted SaaS.
28.summary_security: The server OS is the trust anchor for everything above it; kernel and userland CVEs (3,000+ Linux kernel CVEs in 2024, ~42% high severity) translate directly into RCE, privilege escalation, and ransomware exposure. Banner disclosure aids attackers; suppress Server/X-Powered-By, prefer hardened distros (Alpine, RHEL, minimal Debian), automate patching, apply CIS benchmarks, enable SELinux/AppArmor, and retire EOL releases (CentOS 7, Windows Server 2012).

29.description: Search engines covers on-site search and product-discovery technology embedded in a website: hosted SaaS (Algolia-style, Swiftype, Site Search 360, HawkSearch, Unbxd, Searchanise, Cludo, GroupBy, Attraqt), open-source backends (Elasticsearch-based suites, VuFind, Apisearch), and enterprise platforms (FAST ESP, FAST Search for SharePoint). Visitor queries and click-throughs flow to the search provider for indexing, ranking, and personalisation.
29.name: Search engines
29.summary_privacy: Site-search vendors typically receive query text, user tokens (cookie or hashed ID), obfuscated IP, referrer, and click analytics to power ranking and merchandising. Algolia for example retains userToken analytics 90 days by default (365 with add-ons). Queries themselves can be sensitive (health, legal, identity terms): disclose the vendor in the privacy notice, honour DSAR/erasure on userTokens, tune retention, and never put raw PII in query parameters.
29.summary_security: Most modern site search is SaaS-backed and called from the browser with a public API key, so reflected/stored XSS via unsanitised result rendering, autocomplete injection, and over-privileged API keys (search keys that can list or write indices) recur. Restrict keys to query-only scope, use secured/short-lived keys for personalisation, sanitise highlight snippets, rate-limit, and review the vendor's SOC 2/ISO 27001 posture before exporting catalogues.

30.description: Webmail covers browser-based email clients and hosted email suites detectable on a domain (Google Workspace, Microsoft 365/Outlook Web App, Apple iCloud Mail, Proton Mail, Zimbra, RoundCube, SquirrelMail, RainLoop, Open-Xchange App Suite). Detection usually comes from login portals at /mail, /owa, /webmail, MX records, or vendor-specific markup.
30.name: Webmail
30.summary_privacy: Mailboxes hold the densest personal-data corpus most users have: contracts, ID documents, medical and financial records, intimate correspondence, and password-reset trails for every linked account. Mainstream providers (Google Workspace, Microsoft 365) scan content for spam, search, and AI features unless opted out; E2EE services (Proton Mail) cannot read content. GDPR plus HIPAA, GLBA, FERPA often apply: pick jurisdiction, sign DPA/BAA, encrypt at rest, gate AI training and add-ons.
30.summary_security: Webmail is the highest-value ATO target online: one compromise yields password-reset capability for every linked service plus historical content. Self-hosted clients (RoundCube, SquirrelMail, RainLoop, Zimbra) carry long RCE/XSS/SSRF histories (Zimbra CVE-2022-27924, CVE-2024-45519; Outlook CVE-2024-21413). Enforce FIDO2/WebAuthn MFA, conditional access, disable IMAP/SMTP basic auth, enforce DMARC, gate OAuth add-ons, patch self-hosted clients behind a WAF.

31.description: CDN (Content Delivery Network) covers providers that cache and proxy a site's traffic across globally distributed edge locations - Cloudflare, Akamai, Fastly, Azure CDN, CacheFly, StackPath, Incapsula, Microsoft Ajax CDN, plus regional players (ArvanCloud, Azion, GoCache). They terminate TLS at the edge, accelerate static assets, and frequently bundle DDoS mitigation and WAF features.
31.name: CDN
31.summary_privacy: Because the CDN sees decrypted traffic it has visibility into IPs, URLs, headers, cookies, login bodies, any PII in transit, plus visit metadata. Jurisdiction is decisive: US CLOUD Act, MENA-region or sanctioned operators materially change a site's GDPR Art. 44 / Schrems II posture. Edge-injected scripts (Bot Management, Speed Brain, AB tests) may set IDs pre-consent, intersecting cookie-law. Disclose the CDN as processor, sign DPA with SCCs and TIA, prefer EU/Swiss PoPs.
31.summary_security: CDNs are a deliberate MitM for every visitor: they terminate TLS and a single edge compromise cascades to every fronted site (Cloudbleed 2017, Fastly June 2021, Cloudflare R2 Nov 2024). Common risks are cache poisoning, host-header and Range smuggling, exposed origin IPs, weak origin-side TLS validation, and abuse of edge-injected scripts. Lock origins to CDN IPs, use authenticated origin pulls/mTLS, enforce strict cache keys, pin TLS at edge, monitor cache deception, document off-CDN failover.

32.description: Marketing automation platforms orchestrate email, SMS, push, lead-scoring, customer journeys and behavioural retargeting at scale. They centralise large volumes of identified customer PII (names, emails, phone numbers, purchase history) and deploy client-side tracking pixels, web beacons and identity resolution scripts to correlate user behaviour across sessions and channels. Vendors include HubSpot, Marketo, Mautic, Klaviyo and dozens of niche SaaS players, almost all of which transfer first-party data to their own cloud for processing and onward sharing with sub-processors.
32.name: Marketing automation
32.summary_privacy: Severe - critical scrutiny band. Platforms ingest identified PII (email, phone, address, purchase history, behavioural profiles, lead scores) and deploy first/third-party pixels with persistent identifiers joining the marketing graph across sessions and channels. Data is processed in vendor cloud, US-transferred under SCCs, shared with ad/analytics/enrichment sub-processors. GDPR opt-in + DPA, ePrivacy for trackers, CCPA opt-out, COPPA for minors; sensitive-category inference is routine.
32.summary_security: Moderate-to-high attack surface for a SaaS class. Primary risks: vendor social engineering (Mailchimp Mar 2022 + Jan/Apr 2023, HubSpot Mar 2024, Klaviyo Aug 2022); customer pixel snippets and webhooks that enlarge the JS trust boundary on embedding sites; form-builder and merge-tag features producing template-injection, SSRF and XSS. Controls: vendor MFA/SSO, scoped rotating API keys, pixel CSP review, signed DPA with sub-processor list.

33.description: Web server extensions are server-side modules that add functionality to HTTP servers such as Apache httpd, NGINX, IIS or LiteSpeed. The family includes mod_ssl, mod_dav, mod_rewrite, mod_php, mod_wsgi, mod_perl, mod_jk, mod_fcgid, OpenSSL bindings and similar plug-ins. They execute inside the request-handling process with high privileges, expand the server attack surface, and historically account for a large share of httpd/nginx CVEs (memory corruption, authentication bypass, request smuggling).
33.name: Web server extensions
33.summary_privacy: Low intrinsic privacy risk. Server extensions terminate or process HTTP traffic but do not, by themselves, profile end users. However, access and SSL handshake logs may contain IP addresses and request paths that qualify as personal data under GDPR, so retention, anonymisation and PCI/SOC2 logging discipline still apply.
33.summary_security: High attack surface. These modules execute inside the web server with elevated privileges and have a long CVE history: CVE-2024-40898 mod_ssl auth bypass, CVE-2024-38475 mod_rewrite filesystem disclosure, CVE-2026-42945 nginx ngx_http_rewrite_module RCE, mod_dav crash and SSL session-resumption bugs. Recommended controls: minimal module set, prompt patching, hardened TLS configuration, WAF in front.

34.description: Databases are persistence and query engines that store the authoritative copies of an organisation's data: relational systems (PostgreSQL, MySQL, MariaDB, SQLite, Aurora, FileMaker), NoSQL (MongoDB, Redis, PouchDB, Firebase), search indexes (Solr, Lucene) and managed/data-platform stacks (Cloudera, Percona). They typically hold the most sensitive content in a stack, including credentials, customer PII, payment records, health and financial data. Any direct internet exposure or authentication misconfiguration leads to mass-data compromise.
34.name: Databases
34.summary_privacy: Very high PII concentration. Databases hold the canonical record of personal, financial, health and payment data. Privacy risk derives less from active tracking and more from the magnitude and sensitivity of stored data: a single compromise can trigger GDPR Article 33 notification, HIPAA breach reporting and PCI penalties. Pseudonymisation, field-level encryption and access logging are core mitigations.
34.summary_security: Critical attack surface. Internet-exposed databases are a top breach vector: tens of thousands of misconfigured MongoDB, Redis and Elasticsearch instances are visible on Shodan, and recent CVEs include MongoBleed (CVE-2025-14847) and routine privilege-escalation flaws across PostgreSQL/MySQL. Controls: never expose to the internet, enforce strong auth and TLS, least-privilege roles, encryption at rest, regular patching, and tested backup/restore.

35.description: Map technologies render interactive geographic content in the browser and serve geocoding, routing and tile APIs from the cloud. The category covers Google Maps, Apple MapKit JS, Azure Maps, HERE, TomTom, Mapbox, MapLibre, OpenLayers, ArcGIS, CARTO, Maptiler and many store-locator widgets. They typically load third-party JavaScript and tile requests, transmitting visitor IP address, viewport coordinates, search terms and (with consent) precise geolocation to the map vendor for processing in the United States or other jurisdictions.
35.name: Maps
35.summary_privacy: High privacy impact. Google Maps and most commercial APIs transfer visitor IP, viewport, URL and (on permission) precise geolocation to the vendor in the US, often setting advertising or fingerprinting cookies. CJEU Schrems II and German LDA/LG Munchen rulings held loading Google Maps without prior opt-in violates GDPR. Precise geolocation is sensitive data under several US state laws. Mitigations: consent-gated lazy-load, prefer EU-tile Maptiler/MapLibre or self-hosted OSM, declare in DPA log.
35.summary_security: Moderate attack surface. Hosted SaaS APIs bound risk to (1) JS supply-chain compromise of the map SDK, (2) leaked API tokens reused for service abuse or overage bills, (3) XSS via untrusted marker/info-window HTML. Self-hosted Leaflet/MapLibre/OpenLayers/OSM tile servers add the usual server-side surface. Controls: referrer/IP-scoped rotated keys, SRI-pinned SDKs, sanitised marker content, prefer EU-hosted MapLibre where sovereignty matters.

36.description: Advertising technologies place, auction, target, verify and measure display, video, native and search ads. The category encompasses ad networks, demand-side and supply-side platforms (DSP/SSP), data-management platforms (DMP), real-time-bidding exchanges, ad-fraud and verification vendors, attribution and pixel-based measurement systems. They embed client-side scripts that read cookies, device fingerprints and advertising IDs, then broadcast bidstream payloads with IP, geolocation, browsing history and inferred attributes to dozens of third-party bidders on every impression.
36.name: Advertising
36.summary_privacy: Severe - the canonical critical-band case. RTB broadcasts personal data (IP, precise geo, device IDs, URL, inferred interests, often health/sexuality/political signals) to thousands of bidders per impression. ICCL/Ryan shows users generate hundreds of broadcasts daily. Belgian Market Court May 2025 confirmed TC-strings are personal data; FTC InMarket/X-Mode 2024 banned precise-location sale. GDPR opt-in + per-bidder basis, ePrivacy, CCPA with GPC, COPPA bans behavioural ads to minors.
36.summary_security: Elevated attack surface despite client placement. Risks: (1) JS supply-chain compromise of the bidder chain - polyfill.io (Jun 2024, ~380k hosts) and Magecart skimmers travel through ad networks since every bidder iframe extends the JS trust boundary; (2) malvertising creative-injection delivers drive-by exploits, miners and phishing with no per-bidder vetting; (3) ad-fraud tooling yields CSRF and ATO. Controls: server tagging, CSP+SRI, sandboxed iframes, ads.txt hygiene, bidder review.

37.description: Network device technologies cover infrastructure monitoring, remote administration and management interfaces for routers, switches, firewalls and IT systems, such as Paessler PRTG, TeamViewer/Monitis, SolarWinds, Nagios-class agents and embedded device admin panels. They typically run as privileged services on internal hosts, expose web UIs and APIs, accept SNMP/WMI/SSH/RPC connections to managed assets, and store credentials for every device they manage.
37.name: Network devices
37.summary_privacy: Low intrinsic privacy impact for end users; data plane is mostly operational telemetry. However device logs, NetFlow/sFlow, DPI and remote-screen/session-recording features incidentally collect employee and visitor activity, triggering GDPR Article 88, EDPB Guidelines 5/2020 and labour-law consultation (DE Betriebsrat, FR CSE). Source IPs in firewall and admin-portal logs are personal data under GDPR. Mitigations: minimum retention, log RBAC, purpose limitation, works-council disclosure.
37.summary_security: Critical attack surface - most-exploited per CISA KEV. Routers, firewalls, VPN gateways and privileged monitoring (PRTG, SolarWinds, TeamViewer) sit on the management plane and hold credentials to every managed asset. Mass-exploited zero-days: Cisco IOS XE CVE-2023-20198 (10.0), Citrix Bleed CVE-2023-4966, Palo Alto CVE-2024-3400 (10.0), Fortinet/Ivanti advisories, SolarWinds 2020, TeamViewer 2024 APT29. Controls: no internet-exposed admin UIs, MFA, segmented mgmt VLANs, fast patching.

38.description: Media servers are server-side platforms that ingest, transcode, package, protect and deliver audio and video to end users. The category includes Wowza Streaming Engine, Uplynk, Muvi, AzuraCast (web radio), Ausha and Fireside (podcast hosting), Odeum and Sardius (OTT/live), plus game-streaming portals such as BigPoint. They typically expose RTMP, RTSP, HLS, DASH and web-management interfaces, run as privileged processes, and integrate with CDNs, DRM and ad-insertion services.
38.name: Media servers
38.summary_privacy: Moderate-to-elevated impact. Subscriber accounts hold names, emails, payment references and detailed watch/listen history exposing interests, schedule and household composition - the Plex Aug 2022 breach (~15M emails, usernames, hashed passwords) and role in 2022 LastPass compromise show the cost. Ad-insertion and recommendation features add cookies/device IDs and share telemetry; kids OTT and gaming portals attract COPPA. Mitigations: minimal fields, hashed history, opt-in personalisation.
38.summary_security: High attack surface. Media servers parse complex container/transport/DRM formats, expose admin UIs plus RTMP/RTSP/HLS/DASH endpoints and run privileged. CVEs: Plex CVE-2020-5741 (auth RCE, CISA KEV after 2022 LastPass breach traced to un-patched home Plex); Wowza CVE-2024-52052+52053 chain (unauth XSS-to-root via Manager port 8088, ~18,500 servers exposed); parser bugs across Jellyfin/Emby/Icecast. Controls: no internet-exposed admin UIs, isolated transcoders, stream token auth.

39.description: Webcams covers network-connected camera devices and their associated web interfaces, streaming endpoints, and management software (IP cameras, MJPEG/RTSP/ONVIF streamers, embedded CCTV web UIs, baby/security cameras, computer-vision/streaming-as-a-service offerings). Detection signals include exposed admin panels, ONVIF/RTSP service banners, MJPEG HTTP streams, and vendor-specific firmware fingerprints. Devices in this category are usually long-lived embedded systems with infrequent firmware updates, default credentials, and exposed management interfaces, which makes them a recurring target for IoT botnets such as Mirai, Persirai and Corona-Mirai, and a sensitive PII surface because they capture audio/video of people, homes, and workplaces.
39.name: Webcams
39.summary_privacy: Very high privacy risk: continuous audio/video of homes, workplaces and children, often streamed to vendor clouds. Footage typically qualifies as biometric/special-category data under GDPR Art.9. Misconfigured devices are indexed on Shodan/Insecam exposing live feeds. Vendor incidents (Verkada 2021, Ring police-sharing) and FTC consent decrees against Ring/Genie show platform-side failures. HIPAA, FERPA and COPPA attach in context. Companion apps add analytics SDKs.
39.summary_security: Very high attack surface: embedded firmware, default/weak credentials, exposed RTSP/ONVIF/MJPEG and admin panels, and a long history of mass exploitation (Mirai, Persirai, Corona-Mirai, AVTECH CVE-2024-7029, Edimax CVE-2025-1316). Many devices reach end-of-life without patches and are routinely conscripted into DDoS/Telnet botnets. Treat any exposed camera endpoint as untrusted and assume RCE-grade exposure on the LAN it sits in.

41.description: Payment processors covers gateways, acquirers, orchestrators, tokenization vaults and BNPL/checkout providers that authorize, capture and settle card or alternative-payment transactions on websites and apps (e.g. Stripe, Adyen, PayPal/Braintree, Worldpay, plus regional players such as Conekta, Culqi, HiPay, Klasha and PCI-DSS-scope tooling like PCI Proxy and Feroot). Implementations span hosted iframes/Elements, drop-in widgets, redirect flows and full server-side APIs. The category is regulated by PCI-DSS and processes the most sensitive customer data on a typical site (PAN, CVV, billing identity, sometimes KYC documents).
41.name: Payment processors
41.summary_privacy: Very high PII exposure: PAN, CVV, billing/shipping identity, device fingerprint, and increasingly biometric or government-ID data for KYC. Processors share data with card networks, issuers, fraud-scoring vendors and regulators; many also run analytics or anti-fraud telemetry on behaviour. Subject to GDPR/CCPA as joint or independent controllers and to PCI-DSS, GLBA and regional financial regulators. Vendor choice and field minimisation materially shift merchant exposure.
41.summary_security: Critical: handles cardholder data under PCI-DSS plus fraud/KYC signals. Major processors are Level-1 PCI service providers, but client-side integrations are routinely abused by Magecart skimmers, malicious tag injection and rogue checkout scripts. PCI-DSS 4.0 now mandates script integrity and tamper monitoring on payment pages. Use hosted fields/iframes, strict CSP, SRI, 3DS2, server-side tokenization and continuous monitoring of any third-party JS on checkout.

42.description: Tag managers covers client-side container/loader systems used to inject and orchestrate third-party JavaScript on websites and apps without redeploying the site (Google Tag Manager, Adobe Experience Platform Launch/DTM, Tealium, Matomo Tag Manager, Commanders Act, Ensighten, Yahoo! Tag Manager, etc.). The container itself is typically a small loader script; the real risk surface is the open-ended set of marketing, analytics, advertising and pixel tags fired through it (Meta Pixel, Google Ads, ad-network pixels) and the privileged ability for marketing users to ship arbitrary JS to production.
42.name: Tag managers
42.summary_privacy: Critical: tag managers are the conduit for most third-party tracking, advertising and analytics, often firing before consent. The German Hanover court (2025) ruled GTM loading itself requires prior consent as it contacts Google and exposes IP/device data. Tags routinely leak hashed emails, form contents, URLs and behaviour to ad networks. HIPAA/health-sector use has produced enforcement and breach reports. Expect joint-controller obligations under GDPR.
42.summary_security: Very high attack surface despite a benign loader: tag managers grant marketing users arbitrary-JS injection on every page, including checkout and authenticated areas. Recurring Magecart skimmer conduit; related to the 2024 Polyfill.io supply-chain compromise. Often shipped without CSP, SRI, server-side mode, change review or least-privilege containers. PCI-DSS 4.0 6.4.3 and 11.6.1 (effective 31 March 2025) mandate inventory, authorization and tamper monitoring of any script on payment pages.

44.description: CI (continuous integration) covers build, test and pipeline orchestration platforms used to compile, test and ship code: Jenkins, GitLab CI/CD, GitHub Actions, CircleCI, TeamCity, Bamboo, Drone, Buildkite, Travis CI, plus code-quality bots such as Code Climate. Detection is mostly indirect on public-facing properties (status badges, footer attribution, headers on hosted runners or artifacts) but the systems themselves run server-side with broad access to source code, signing keys, deploy credentials, cloud IAM tokens and production environments.
44.name: CI
44.summary_privacy: Low direct privacy risk: CI systems normally process source code, test data and build artefacts rather than end-user PII, and do not run in the user's browser. However, repos and test fixtures sometimes contain real customer data, API tokens or PII, and hosted CI providers process developer identity data subject to GDPR. Logs and artefacts can persist secrets. Third-party plugins, actions and orbs are an opaque data-sharing channel that should be inventoried.
44.summary_security: Very high attack surface and blast radius: CI holds long-lived secrets, signing keys and cloud credentials and executes arbitrary build code, making it a prime supply-chain target. Major incidents: SolarWinds (build tampering), Codecov bash uploader (2021), 2025 tj-actions/changed-files and reviewdog/action-setup GitHub Actions compromises leaking CI secrets. ~24% of supply-chain attacks abuse CI/CD. Controls: OIDC short tokens, pinned SHAs, isolated runners, SBOM, provenance.

45.description: Control systems covers operational-technology (OT) and industrial-control-system style management interfaces: SCADA HMIs, BMS/BAS dashboards, PLC/RTU web UIs, energy/HVAC controllers, fleet- and asset-management consoles and similar IT-OT bridge tools. On the public web these tend to surface as exposed web admin panels, REST APIs, or vendor portals; the underlying systems run server-side or on embedded infrastructure. Even when the protected asset is a physical process, the web tier is increasingly an HTTP/HTTPS application reachable from the internet, and is the most common initial-access vector.
45.name: Control systems
45.summary_privacy: Low direct privacy footprint: mostly telemetry, sensor and process data, not consumer PII or behavioural tracking. Personal data, when present, is usually operator/employee accounts, building-access logs or camera feeds integrated into BMS, which can implicate GDPR. Trackers and third-party ad/analytics are rare on OT UIs. The dominant regulatory concern is not privacy law but sector cyber regulation (NIS2, NERC CIP, IEC 62443, CISA sector guidance).
45.summary_security: Maximum attack surface: control-system web interfaces govern physical processes; compromise can yield kinetic damage or wide-area outage. Legacy stacks, default credentials, flat networks and exposed protocols are endemic. Stuxnet (2010) and Triton/Trisis (2017) remain canonical. CISA/FBI warn of state-aligned pre-positioning by Volt Typhoon in US water, energy and telecom OT (AA24-038A). Controls: Purdue segmentation, MFA, no direct internet exposure, ZTNA, IEC 62443.

46.description: Remote access covers tools that grant administrative or end-user access to remote machines/networks: RMM and remote-desktop products (TeamViewer, AnyDesk, ScreenConnect/ConnectWise, Atera, Netop, Impero, Glance), enterprise VPN/ZTNA gateways (Citrix/NetScaler, Palo Alto GlobalProtect, Pulse Secure), out-of-band hardware management (HP iLO, Intel AMT), co-browsing/screen-sharing widgets (Upscope, Cardina), and reverse web terminals (ShellInABox). On the web tier they surface as login portals, agent endpoints and management consoles; the underlying agents run with high or system privilege on the controlled hosts.
46.name: Remote access
46.summary_privacy: High risk by capability, not tracking: an unattended remote session can observe screen, files, webcam, keystrokes and stored credentials. Co-browsing/screen-share widgets in support flows may capture PII shown on the agent's view, including health and financial data. Vendor session-recording features can store screen snippets in the provider cloud. Trackers on operational endpoints are rare; mobile clients and marketing sites add the usual analytics SDKs.
46.summary_security: Maximum attack surface: compromise yields full interactive control of remote endpoints. Incident history: 2024 AnyDesk source/cert breach (signed Agent Tesla), APT29 TeamViewer corporate breach, ConnectWise ScreenConnect CVE-2024-1708/1709 used by Storm-1175 for Medusa ransomware, Citrix NetScaler chain (CVE-2019-19781, 2023-3519, 2025-7775). CISA/FBI/MS-ISAC repeatedly warn of RMM abuse for initial access. Require MFA, allow-lists, JIT access, agent integrity and rapid patching.

47.description: Development is a broad bucket of developer-facing tools surfaced on websites and SaaS portals: source-code browsers and forges (Forgejo, Gitiles, gitlist, Phabricator, Atlassian FishEye), cloud IDEs (Acquia Cloud IDE), low-code/no-code builders (Betty Blocks, genezio, MindStudio, Stammer.ai), front-end/runtime libraries (styled-components, Opal, MichiJS, CheerPJ), API and documentation tools (Apiary), and a long tail of niche dev SaaS. The category is heterogeneous; some entries are pure client-side libraries with minimal runtime exposure, while others are full developer platforms with code, secrets and customer data behind them.
47.name: Development
47.summary_privacy: Moderate: most tools handle developer identity, code, issues and project metadata rather than end-user PII, but cloud IDEs and AI code assistants transmit proprietary code, prompts and editor context to vendor APIs, creating an exfiltration pathway. No-code platforms host customer-facing apps that themselves collect PII, inheriting GDPR/CCPA obligations downstream. Trackers on the marketing fronts of dev SaaS are common; operational interfaces usually run only first-party analytics.
47.summary_security: Variable but materially elevated. Self-hosted forges (Phabricator, Gitiles, Forgejo, Gitea) expose source, secrets, tokens and carry RCE/auth-bypass CVEs. Cloud IDEs and AI editors broaden the surface: 2024-2025 saw critical flaws in popular VS Code and Cursor extensions (128M+ installs) and Cursor allowed silent code execution on cloned repos. AI coding agents introduce prompt-injection paths. Front-end libraries inherit npm supply-chain risk. Pin extensions, keep dev tools behind SSO/MFA.

48.description: Network storage encompasses Network Attached Storage (NAS) appliances, distributed file systems, and cloud file services (e.g., Synology DiskStation, QNAP, Amazon EFS, Red Hat Gluster, IPFS) that expose file shares over LAN or WAN protocols (SMB, NFS, S3, iSCSI). These systems hold the bulk of an organization's unstructured data, often including PII, intellectual property, and backups. They are infrastructure components rather than client-facing widgets, so tracker density is essentially zero, but they present a very high-value target for ransomware and credential-stuffing attacks—Synology and QNAP devices have a long history of CVE exploitation (eCh0raix, DeadBolt, CVE-2021-28799, multiple 2025 Pwn2Own zero-days). Internet-exposed admin panels remain the dominant breach vector.
48.name: Network storage
48.summary_privacy: Privacy risk is moderate-to-high: stores typically hold large concentrations of PII, source code, HR, healthcare and finance records, and backups, yet the systems are not data brokers and embed virtually no trackers. SaaS variants (Amazon EFS, Azure Files) inherit the cloud provider's data-handling regime; self-hosted NAS keeps data on-prem. The dominant privacy threat is breach-driven disclosure, so encryption-at-rest, access logging, and prompt GDPR/CCPA/HIPAA breach notification are key.
48.summary_security: Very high attack surface: internet-exposed NAS (Synology, QNAP, Asustor, TerraMaster) are repeatedly hit by ransomware (eCh0raix, DeadBolt, Checkmate) and chained at Pwn2Own. Pwn2Own Ireland 2025 burned seven QNAP zero-days (CVE-2025-62847/62848/62849 + HBS 3 chains) and two Synology DSM zero-days, all enabling unauthenticated RCE and full filesystem takeover. Weak admin creds, stale firmware, WAN-facing UIs remain primary vectors. Controls: patching, MFA, segmentation, immutable snapshots.

49.description: Feed readers aggregate content from RSS, Atom, and JSON feeds so users can follow many sources from one interface. Examples in this taxonomy range from cloud platforms (Feedly, Inoreader, AnnounceKit, Beehiiv RSS, Blendle) to self-hosted aggregators (Planet, Miniflux-style projects). Cloud readers necessarily store users' subscription lists, reading history, and identity, which constitutes a behavioral profile. Most readers are server-side rendered with thin web/mobile clients; tracker injection is typically light. Self-hosted feed aggregators have a very small attack surface because they generally only make outbound HTTP fetches. The category overall is low-risk relative to other web technologies on the site.
49.name: Feed readers
49.summary_privacy: Privacy exposure is modest. Cloud feed readers (Feedly, Inoreader, AnnounceKit) log subscription lists, IP, and reading patterns; some inject Google Analytics or Mixpanel and may share anonymized engagement data with ad networks. The data is interest-revealing but not financial or health-grade. Self-hosted aggregators eliminate third-party transfer entirely. GDPR and cookie-law apply to EU users, but PII exposure is limited to email/name plus reading behavior.
49.summary_security: Low attack surface overall: feed readers mostly make outbound HTTP fetches with no inbound exposure. Server-side fetchers can be tricked into SSRF or XXE if XML parsers are misconfigured, and OPML uploads occasionally introduce parsing bugs, but documented exploits are rare and impact is generally limited to the reader's own account. Cloud accounts can leak via credential reuse; self-hosted instances inherit the operator's hardening posture.

50.description: Document Management Systems (DMS) ingest, store, version, and route business documents—contracts, HR files, financial records, medical records, scholarly assets—through workflows with role-based access, audit trails, and retention policies. The category includes academic repositories (DSpace, Invenio, Koha, Open Journal Systems), enterprise file/collaboration platforms (Clinked, Onehub), domain-specific systems (Paperless Pipeline for real estate, uKnowva HR), e-signature platforms (Clicksign), and knowledge tools (Evernote). DMS are predominantly server-side SaaS with browser/desktop clients. Their core value—centralized custody of sensitive documents—makes PII exposure a defining risk, while comprehensive audit and access controls are core features used to satisfy GDPR, HIPAA, and SOC 2.
50.name: DMS
50.summary_privacy: Very high PII concentration: contracts, ID scans, medical records, HR files, financial statements. Vendors typically act as data processors under GDPR; cross-border transfers, sub-processor lists, and retention defaults need scrutiny. Third-party trackers are usually limited to marketing pages, not document views. Key controls are encryption at rest, field-level/document-level ACLs, granular audit logs, and configurable retention.
50.summary_security: High attack surface: DMS expose authenticated upload/preview pipelines, search indexers, and document parsers (PDF, Office) with long RCE history, plus sharing links and OAuth integrations creating unauthorized access paths. 2024 Dropbox Sign breach and recurring SharePoint preview/ID-chain CVEs show even mature vendors are routinely exploited; self-hosted (DSpace, Koha, Invenio) inherit Java/PHP web-app risks. Breach impact is severe: DMS is the canonical store of sensitive documents.

51.description: Page builders are visual, drag-and-drop tools that let non-developers compose websites and landing pages. The category spans WordPress builders (Elementor-adjacent, Oxygen, LiveCanvas, Etch, Level 5), hosted no-code platforms (Pagecloud, JouwWeb, Flexbe, MailerLite Website Builder, Heyflow), headless/composable builders (Stackbit, Netlify Create, Makeswift, Frontastic), and niche industry builders. Output is mixed: builders run server-side authoring tools but emit client-side HTML, JS, and a tag-laden rendering layer. The main inherent risk on the WordPress side is plugin sprawl—Elementor and Divi have repeatedly shipped critical RCE, XSS, and authorization-bypass vulnerabilities. SaaS builders bundle marketing trackers and frequently inject third-party widgets (forms, chat, analytics) into the published sites.
51.name: Page builders
51.summary_privacy: Privacy risk is moderate. Builders collect editor-account PII and visitor analytics, and typically encourage embedding Google Analytics, Meta Pixel, chat widgets, and form processors so the published site ships a non-trivial tracker payload. Visitor data captured by bundled form widgets is often forwarded to the vendor's SaaS endpoint without a clear DPA path. GDPR consent management is the site owner's responsibility, not the builder's, which routinely leads to non-compliance by default.
51.summary_security: High attack surface in the WordPress builder ecosystem. Critical RCEs hit widely deployed builders yearly: CVE-2024-25600 Bricks unauth RCE 9.8 actively exploited within days; CVE-2025-6327/6325 King Addons for Elementor unauth upload + privesc; CVE-2025-30911 RomethemeKit subscriber RCE; CVE-2023-48777 Elementor template-import RCE. Divi has shipped CSRF and stored XSS. Hosted no-code builders are more contained but expose authoring APIs and form endpoints. Plugin sprawl compounds risk.

52.description: Live chat is a client-side widget plus server-side messaging backend that lets website visitors converse with operators or AI agents in real time. The category covers generalist platforms (LiveChat, Zendesk Sunshine Conversations, Intercom-style tools, ClickDesk, WhosOn, Atlasmic), vertical solutions (Klara and Medchat for healthcare, ChatFood and Akia for hospitality, Glia for financial services), AI chatbots (MoinAI, Floatbot, Jenny, Solvemate, EliseAI, Gista), and messaging-app bridges (GetButton, Pancake, GetChat). The widget runs on the page, sets cookies, and ships visitor metadata plus full chat content to a third-party processor. Chats commonly contain unstructured PII—names, emails, phone numbers, account IDs, health details, payment hints—pasted by users with no redaction. This makes the category one of the highest-privacy-sensitivity client-side technologies.
52.name: Live chat
52.summary_privacy: Critical privacy exposure: visitors paste PII (sometimes credentials) into chats, and vendors retain transcripts, IP, UA, geo, and behavioural data for analytics and ML training. Transcripts are routinely shared with sub-processors for translation, sentiment, LLM summarisation, and ad enrichment with limited transparency. GDPR requires lawful basis, DPA, and explicit consent for non-essential cookies; many fall short. HIPAA chats need BAAs; generic widgets on healthcare sites are an HHS target.
52.summary_security: High attack surface and very high blast radius. Chat widgets run with full DOM access on the host, so vendor compromise becomes supply-chain XSS / session theft across every embedding site. Aug 2025 Salesloft/Drift (UNC6395) abused Drift OAuth tokens to exfiltrate Salesforce data from 700+ orgs (Cloudflare, Google, Palo Alto, Proofpoint, Tanium, Zscaler), hunting AWS keys, Snowflake tokens, VPN creds in case fields. Agent consoles are phishing targets; OAuth/CRM integrations widen exposure.

53.description: Customer Relationship Management (CRM) systems centralize contacts, accounts, deals, communications, and transaction history—the canonical store of an organization's customer PII. The taxonomy includes generalist CRMs (CiviCRM), vertical CRMs (Brivity, Gestim, MRI Box and Dice for real estate; Tessitura for arts; Klara, ClinicSense, TDO for healthcare; Arketa, Virtuagym for fitness; Roller for entertainment; Giveffect, Fluro for nonprofits; Ellucian for higher-ed), and AI-driven engagement layers (Bowtie, Proto AICX, EliseAI, Chatlyn). CRMs are predominantly server-side SaaS accessed via web and API. They concentrate highly sensitive identifiers (name, email, phone, address, sometimes SSN, DOB, payment-card hints, health context) and are repeatedly named in major breaches (Salesforce/Salesloft, Gainsight, Cisco). Because the data is both PII-rich and commercially valuable, CRMs are top-tier ransomware and credential-theft targets.
53.name: CRM
53.summary_privacy: Critical privacy exposure: a CRM is by definition a PII database. GDPR rights (access, erasure, portability), CCPA opt-out, sector laws (HIPAA, GLBA, FERPA) apply directly. Vendors act as processors under detailed DPAs; sub-processor sprawl (enrichment, email, analytics, AI training, CS tools) is now the dominant breach vector, as the 2025 Salesloft/Drift and Gainsight incidents showed. Product-UI trackers are limited; the threat is downstream sharing, AI-training posture, and breach disclosure.
53.summary_security: High attack surface, very high blast radius. CRMs expose REST/GraphQL APIs, OAuth marketplaces, complex permissions. 2025 saw two mass third-party-OAuth incidents on Salesforce: Aug 2025 Salesloft/Drift (UNC6395) exfiltrated 700+ tenants (Cloudflare, Google, Palo Alto, Proofpoint, Tanium, Zscaler); Oct-Nov 2025 Gainsight hit 200+ (ShinyHunters claims ~1000). Weaknesses: over-permissive tokens, broad-scope plugins, weak MFA, IDOR, unrestricted exports, secrets in case fields.

54.description: Search Engine Optimization (SEO) covers the audit, content, and analytics tools that help sites rank in search engines and AI answer engines. The taxonomy mixes WordPress plugins (Yoast, RankMath, SEOPress, The SEO Framework, SEOmatic for Craft), audit/rank-tracking SaaS (BrightEdge, BrightLocal, Web CEO, Whitespark, SEOmator, Atomseo), local-listing managers (Synup, RioSEO, SiteBooster, On The Map), structured-data and AI-content tools (WordLift, WP SEO AI, Huckabuy, BlogHunch, Alli), and translation/internationalization tools (Linguana). Most ship a JavaScript snippet, a tracking pixel, or an injection of structured data into the host page; some maintain large clickstream pipelines built from ISP and browser-extension data. Tracker injection and third-party data sharing are the defining privacy concerns; attack surface is moderate.
54.name: SEO
54.summary_privacy: Privacy risk is moderate but tracker likelihood is high: most SaaS SEO tools embed marketing tags and syndicate clickstream data sourced from ISPs, browser extensions, and panel data (e.g., Semrush methodology). Injected snippets can persist cookies and beacon visitor identifiers to the vendor. PII is mostly limited to account holders plus aggregated visitor metrics, but cross-site behavioral profiling via clickstream partners triggers GDPR and ePrivacy/cookie-law obligations.
54.summary_security: Moderate attack surface. WordPress SEO plugins (Yoast, RankMath, SEOPress) have produced multiple stored-XSS and privilege-escalation CVEs over the years; they run with admin-level access to post content and meta. SaaS dashboards expose Search Console / Google Analytics OAuth tokens, which become high-value targets. Site-injected snippets create a supply-chain XSS path on every page. Beyond that, SEO tools rarely process payments or core PII, so blast radius is contained.

55.description: Accounting technologies are software platforms and services used by businesses and accounting firms to manage bookkeeping, invoicing, payroll, tax preparation, expense tracking, financial reporting, equity management, and lending operations. Representative vendors include QuickBooks, Xero, Sage, Carta, Epicor, Akaunting, Taxdome, Ignition, Liscio, Tiller, and Iress.
