These systems are server-hosted SaaS or on-premise products that process highly sensitive financial records (bank accounts, ACH details, tax IDs, payroll, payment-card data, social-security numbers) and personally identifiable information for both businesses and individuals. They are heavily targeted by phishing, business-email-compromise, ACH-fraud, and credential-stuffing campaigns and are typically subject to SOC2, PCI-DSS, GDPR/CCPA, and (in the US) GLBA financial-privacy obligations.
55.name: Accounting
55.summary_privacy: Processes maximum-sensitivity PII: names, SSNs/tax IDs, addresses, bank account/routing numbers, payment-card data, salary, beneficial-ownership and client equity records. Falls under GDPR/CCPA, GLBA (US financial-privacy) and PCI-DSS where card data is present. Marketing trackers may appear but the application core should not share personal data with third parties without a DPA and sub-processor list. Verify data-residency, retention windows and sub-processor disclosures.
55.summary_security: High-value attack target: stores bank credentials, ACH/wire details, tax IDs and payroll data. 2025 saw widespread credential-stuffing of QuickBooks Online with attackers adding fraudulent vendor ACH records, plus phishing impersonating UltraTax/Thomson Reuters. Breach economics severe (average finance breach >$6M). Required controls: SOC2 Type II, MFA, RBAC, encryption at rest/in transit, audit logging, restricted API tokens, anti-fraud monitoring on vendor/payroll changes.

56.description: Cryptominers in the web-technology context are in-browser JavaScript cryptocurrency-mining scripts (Coinhive, CoinHive Captcha, Crypto-Loot, Coinimp, JSEcoin, deepMiner, Minero.cc, Coinhave) and related mining-pool/management services (e.g., Minerstat). When embedded on a website they hijack visitor CPU/GPU to mine Monero, uPlexa or similar coins, almost always without informed consent, a practice known as cryptojacking. Coinhive (shut down March 2019) at its peak ran on hundreds of thousands of sites, and the technique resurged in 2025 with WebSocket-based, obfuscated variants compromising 3,500+ sites. Even after Coinhive shutdown, copy-cat services and supply-chain injections via compromised plugins, CDNs, and ad networks continue.
56.name: Cryptominers
56.summary_privacy: Direct PII collection is limited (IP, UA, site key) but the script is third-party JS in the first-party origin and can exfil cookies, local storage, session tokens and form data at will - observed doing so in 2025 campaigns. Consent is essentially never obtained, violating GDPR/ePrivacy lawful-basis requirements and consumer-protection laws. Browsers, AV and ad-blockers classify these scripts as malware; tracker/malware blocklist listing (uBlock, Disconnect, NoCoin) is standard.
56.summary_security: Almost universally malicious. In-browser miners run attacker JS that exhausts visitor CPU/GPU and serves as a beachhead; many persist via pop-unders or WebSockets after tab close. 2025 campaigns (3500+ hijacked sites, cside research) chain miners with Magecart skimmers from the same domains. Injected via compromised npm packages, plugins, CDNs or stolen ad inventory. Presence signals cryptojacking or supply-chain compromise; immediate blocking and IR warranted.

57.description: Static site generators (SSGs) are build-time tools that compile content (commonly Markdown plus templates) into plain HTML/CSS/JS that can be served directly from any web server or CDN. Mainstream examples include Hugo, Jekyll, Astro, Docusaurus, Next.js SSG, Zola, Lume, Bridgetown, Publii, Scully, Octopress, Cecil, Retype, Rspress, Saber and Sourcey. Because the output is a static bundle there is no application server, database, or user session at runtime, so the runtime attack surface is dramatically smaller than a dynamic CMS. SSGs are predominantly open-source, developer-tooling-grade software used for documentation sites, blogs, marketing pages, and Jamstack apps.
57.name: Static site generator
57.summary_privacy: Pure SSG output is informational HTML and does not, by itself, collect PII, set authentication cookies, or persist user data. Privacy exposure comes almost entirely from optional add-ons embedded into the generated pages (analytics, comments, fonts, CDNs, embedded forms, chat). Operators should review which third-party scripts are pulled into the static bundle and apply cookie-law/GDPR consent only where those embeds warrant it. As a category the SSG itself is privacy-neutral.
57.summary_security: Runtime attack surface is minimal: no app server, database or auth at the served origin. Real risk shifts to the build pipeline and supply chain: malicious Jekyll plugins, compromised Hugo/Astro themes, GitHub-Action token exfiltration, leaked S3/CI credentials and unsanitised Markdown. Hugo and peers ship default renderer sandboxing. Sites should still enforce HTTPS, SRI for third-party scripts, security headers (CSP, HSTS) and a hardened pipeline (pinned deps, locked Actions).

58.description: User-onboarding / digital-adoption platforms (DAPs) inject in-app tours, tooltips, checklists, surveys, NPS prompts, and in many cases full session-replay and behavioural analytics into customer-facing or workforce-facing web and mobile apps. Representative vendors include Pendo, Appcues, WalkMe, Whatfix, Userpilot, Chameleon, Userflow, Userlane, Stonly, Toonimo, Arengu, Elevio, Hansel, LOU, Instabot, Poper, Checkin, FintechOS, Canopy Connect, and PeerPal. They typically deploy as a third-party JavaScript SDK (or mobile SDK) loaded into the host application; events, page DOM, screen content and identifying attributes are streamed to the vendor cloud for product analytics, segmentation, A/B testing and replay.
58.name: User onboarding
58.summary_privacy: High-impact privacy category. Element names, button text, page titles and (with session replay) full screen video can capture PII unless masking is explicitly configured. Pendo/WalkMe/peers offer GDPR/CCPA/HIPAA support, but compliance depends on operators enabling masking, signing DPAs and honoring Do-Not-Process. Persistent visitor IDs/cookies are set; events stream to third-party SaaS. Consent banners, sub-processor disclosure and per-page replay scoping are essential.
58.summary_security: Loads vendor JS with broad DOM access in the authenticated host app: same-origin XSS risk if vendor/CDN is compromised. Mature vendors (Pendo, WalkMe, Whatfix) hold SOC2 Type II with regional residency and Do-Not-Process flags. Risks concentrate on misconfig: unmasked replay fields, leaked app keys, over-broad identification. Pin SDKs, use SRI, enforce CSP. 2025 OCR HIPAA enforcement targets in-browser tracking/replay - healthcare use requires a BAA and verified masking.

59.description: Generic client-side JavaScript libraries: utility, UI, animation, layout, charting/visualisation, templating, and helper packages such as Zepto, p5.js, Sigma.js, Pannellum, Muuri, Granim.js, Headroom.js, Darkmode.js, Goober, Enquire.js, Infinite Scroll, JsRender/JsViews/JsObservable, Mavo, SimplexNoise.js, Dynamics.js, Swiffy Slider and DHTMLX. These are open-source or commercial libraries developers add to a page (via bundlers or directly from a CDN) to provide visual or interactive functionality. They run entirely in the user's browser and, by themselves, do not phone home or collect analytics; their security profile is dominated by supply-chain risk (npm and CDN compromise) and known XSS-class vulnerabilities in unpatched versions.
59.name: JavaScript libraries
59.summary_privacy: Most libraries do not by design collect personal data, set tracking cookies or contact remote servers at runtime. Exposure arises when (a) the page loads them from a third-party CDN (sees IP/referrer, may set cookies), (b) the library is compromised and turned into an exfil vector, or (c) the library is itself a fingerprinter (FingerprintJS, ClientJS) or perf-tracker (Boomerang). Self-hosting plus SRI eliminates most exposure; cookie-law transparency applies wherever CDN loads occur.
59.summary_security: Mostly benign in isolation, but supply-chain risk dominates: 2024 polyfill.io takeover hit 100k+ sites; 2025 npm phishing trojanised chalk/debug/ansi-styles (2.6B weekly DLs) and axios (100M); Aug 2025 Nx s1ngularity used an LLM postinstall to exfil secrets; jQuery carries exploited XSS (CVE-2020-11023). Mitigate via pinned versions, SRI on CDN assets, lockfile verification, `npm ci --ignore-scripts`. The bucket also contains active fingerprinters (FingerprintJS, ClientJS).

60.description: Container technologies package applications with their dependencies into portable, isolated runtime units. Representative entries include Docker (the de-facto container runtime), Proxmox VE (virtualisation/containerisation platform), Harbor (OSS image registry with vulnerability scanning, RBAC, and signing), and adjacent infrastructure platforms. Containers run server-side or in infrastructure roles and are not visible to website visitors directly; their security posture matters because they host the application stacks that process user data. Their canonical risks are mis-exposed Docker daemons (TCP socket on 2375), mounted /var/run/docker.sock inside untrusted containers, weak Kubernetes RBAC, exposed dashboards/API servers, and vulnerable base images.
60.name: Containers
60.summary_privacy: Containers themselves do not collect or transmit end-user data; privacy obligations arise from the workloads they host. Image labels, env vars and registry logs can leak secrets but rarely PII. SOC2, HIPAA, PCI-DSS and GDPR apply transitively: the orchestration layer must enforce encryption at rest/in transit, isolate tenants/PHI workloads, retain audit logs and store secrets in a vault rather than env vars. Public registries can leak proprietary data but rarely personal data.
60.summary_security: Attack surface is operator-driven. Common misconfigs: Docker daemon TCP exposed unauthenticated (host takeover), docker.sock mounted in workloads (root on host), privileged/host-network containers, exposed Kubernetes Dashboard or API server, over-broad RBAC and unpatched/untrusted images. Recent CVEs (Docker CVE-2026-34040 authz bypass) and image-supply-chain attacks demand image signing (Cosign), scanning (Harbor/Trivy), least-privilege RBAC, network policies and runtime monitoring.

62.description: Platform-as-a-Service (PaaS) offerings provide managed application runtimes, build pipelines, databases, and edge networking so customers can deploy code without operating servers directly. Representative vendors in this category include Heroku-style and modern PaaS (Acquia Cloud Platform for Drupal, WordPress VIP, wp.cloud, Lagoon for Kubernetes, Gigalixir for Elixir/Phoenix, Deta, 8base, PlatformOS, Zeabur, Brimble, Tiiny Host, Starhost, Bask Health, Mirus, Pxxl, Ocea, Chabokan), regional providers (UKFast, ANS, Nexcess, Bernet Cloud), specialty platforms (Akamai Connected Cloud, ThingPark Enterprise IoT, OXID eShop EE) and low-code PaaS (Betty Blocks). They are multi-tenant by default with optional isolated private spaces; customer workloads run on shared compute, storage, and networking managed by the provider.
62.name: PaaS
62.summary_privacy: PaaS itself typically processes only operational metadata (build/request logs, account info) but the hosted app can store arbitrary PII, payment data or PHI, making the PaaS a GDPR/CCPA processor or sub-processor. Require DPAs, sub-processor lists, regional residency, encryption at rest/in transit and retention policies. Env vars and CI/CD logs are an underrated exfil channel (2026 Vercel incident). Established platforms publish certifications and DPF/SCC; small/regional PaaS vary.
62.summary_security: Shared-responsibility: provider secures platform, customer secures app/secrets/IAM. Multi-tenant runtimes carry noisy-neighbour and lateral-movement risk; reputable providers (Heroku, Vercel, Netlify, Akamai) offer private tiers and SOC2/PCI/HIPAA attestations. Platform-stored secrets are the lethal blast radius: Apr 2022 Heroku/Travis OAuth-token theft hit npm and GitHub orgs; Apr 2026 Vercel breach exposed unencrypted env vars (sold on BreachForums); Aug 2025 Nx s1ngularity LLM exfil.

63.description: Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS) providers deliver foundational compute, storage, and networking primitives on which most of the modern web is built. The category spans hyperscalers (AWS, Google Cloud, Azure), regional clouds (Alibaba, Leaseweb, DigitalOcean), and niche/sovereign IaaS vendors. IaaS shifts the customer's responsibility upward in the shared-responsibility model: the provider secures the physical/host layer while the tenant remains accountable for IAM, object-storage ACLs, network egress rules, and OS-level hardening. Misconfiguration of these tenant-owned controls (open S3 buckets, over-permissive IAM, exposed management ports) is by far the dominant breach vector. Because IaaS hosts production workloads and persistent data, every other category in the stack inherits a dependency on IaaS posture.
63.name: IaaS
63.summary_privacy: IaaS providers act as processors, not trackers, and embed no third-party tags. They hold vast customer PII; lawful-access posture (US CLOUD Act, China cybersecurity law, Schrems II SCC) shapes compliance. Audit DPAs, region pinning, customer-managed keys (CMEK/BYOK), and sub-processor lists. Cross-border transfer is the dominant privacy concern, not behavioural tracking. Tenant misconfig (open buckets, public snapshots) is the realistic PII-leak vector.
63.summary_security: IaaS is the substrate for nearly all customer data; misconfiguration drives the bulk of cloud breaches (public buckets, excessive IAM, open mgmt ports). IBM 2025 Cost of a Data Breach: multi-environment breaches average USD 5.05M. Shared-responsibility puts IAM, network ACLs, and KMS on the tenant. Platform-side: Azure ChaosDB (2021 Cosmos DB key leak), Snowflake 2024 (UNC5537 abused stolen creds against 165 tenants lacking MFA). SOC2/ISO27001 universal.

64.description: Reverse proxies sit between clients and backend applications, terminating TLS, routing, caching, applying WAF policies, and abstracting service topology. Members include Nginx, Envoy, HAProxy, F5 BIG-IP, IBM DataPower, Kong API Gateway, and EZproxy. They are pure infrastructure - they observe every request and response, including auth tokens, session cookies, and decrypted payloads, which makes them a high-value target. Vulnerabilities in this layer (e.g. NGINX CVE-2026-42945 'Rift' heap overflow with CVSS 9.2, HTTP request smuggling, FrontJacking, header-injection desyncs between proxy and origin) have produced critical mass-exploitation events. The plane carries no end-user identifiers of its own but mediates all traffic.
64.name: Reverse proxies
64.summary_privacy: Proxies do not embed marketing trackers, but they handle decrypted PII, auth cookies, and bearer tokens in memory and logs. Risk concentrates in access-log retention, log-shipping pipelines, and any SSL-bumping for security telemetry. Cloud-managed proxies (Cloudflare, Fastly, Akamai, Front Door) are third-party processors under DPA; anycast can route EU traffic through non-EU PoPs unless pinned (Schrems II). Privacy posture depends on operator config, not the proxy software.
64.summary_security: Reverse proxies see every cleartext request after TLS termination - high-value targets and frequent critical CVE subjects. Recent: F5 BIG-IP CVE-2023-46747 (CVSS 9.8 AJP-smuggling RCE, mass-exploited by UNC5174); HAProxy CVE-2023-25725/25950 (request smuggling); NGINX CVE-2022-41741 (mp4). Cloudflare 18 Nov 2025 outage (~4h, bot-mgmt config) showed proxy control-plane blast radius without a CVE. Misconfig (CORS, headers, host validation) enables origin bypass.

65.description: Load balancers distribute inbound traffic across backend pools to provide availability, performance, and elasticity. The category is dominated by cloud-native managed services (AWS ALB/ELB/NLB, Google Cloud Load Balancing, Azure Front Door, Application Request Routing for IIS). Functionally similar to reverse proxies but optimised for L4/L7 distribution, health checking, and cross-zone failover. They commonly perform SSL/TLS termination, which moves the trust boundary onto the LB and decrypts payloads at that hop; data between LB and backend may then traverse the cloud fabric unencrypted unless re-encryption is configured. Like reverse proxies, LBs see all auth headers, session identifiers, and request bodies but rarely embed any trackers.
65.name: Load balancers
65.summary_privacy: Managed LBs are processors under provider DPAs and do not run third-party trackers. They terminate TLS and log client IP, user-agent, path, and request sizes - all PII under GDPR. Access logs typically land in cloud storage (S3, GCS) and need retention/encryption controls. Anycast LB topologies (Front Door, CloudFront, GCLB) can route EU traffic through non-EU PoPs unless region-pinned, raising Schrems II issues. Tenant configuration determines exposure.
65.summary_security: Load balancers are central to availability; misconfig (legacy TLS, weak ciphers, open SGs) is the routine risk. SSL termination shifts trust onto the LB; cleartext flows downstream unless backend HTTPS is enforced. Appliance LBs carry severe CVE history: CitrixBleed (CVE-2023-4966) leaked NetScaler session tokens and was mass-exploited by LockBit 3.0 (Boeing, Comcast Xfinity; CISA AA23-325A). Cloud LBs inherit platform incidents; L7 features (sticky cookies, JWT/OIDC) raise exposure.

66.description: UI frameworks and component libraries provide pre-built CSS/JS primitives for building consistent user interfaces. The category is broad and mostly low-risk: minimal CSS toolkits (Milligram, Simple.css, siimple), government design systems (USWDS, GOV.UK, NSW), framework-specific kits (shadcn-svelte, MDUI, MetroUI, VKUI), and commercial component vendors. The vast majority are static assets - stylesheets and component code - that load no third-party endpoints and observe no user behaviour. The realistic risk surface is supply-chain: a compromised npm package, hijacked CDN, or malicious post-install script in the dependency tree. CSS itself can in rare cases be weaponised for clickjacking or exfiltration, but the framework code itself does not collect data.
66.name: UI frameworks
66.summary_privacy: Locally hosted UI frameworks have near-zero privacy implications: no network calls, no cookies, no identifiers. Risk emerges only when loaded from a third-party CDN (jsDelivr, unpkg, cdnjs), which sees client IP and Referer - a low-grade tracker. Some commercial kits phone home for telemetry/licence checks; OSS rarely does. Government design systems (USWDS, GOV.UK, NSW) are privacy-neutral. The category does not collect PII or share data with third parties under normal use.
66.summary_security: UI frameworks are typically static client assets; the dominant 2025-2026 risk is npm supply-chain. Sep 2025 qix phishing cascaded into chalk/debug/ansi-styles (~2.6B weekly DLs); Shai-Hulud was the first self-propagating npm worm (500+ packages); CISA AA25-266A; late-2025 TanStack typo-squat. Libraries with dangerouslySetInnerHTML escape hatches can amplify XSS. CSS-only frameworks have minimal direct surface. SRI, lockfile pinning, npm provenance, and CSP mitigate most concerns.

67.description: Cookie compliance / Consent Management Platforms (CMPs) are the banner-and-preference widgets that record GDPR/ePrivacy/CCPA consent and gate downstream tracker firing. Members include OneTrust, Cookiebot (Usercentrics), Axeptio, Consent Manager, CrownPeak, Clym, TrustCommander, TRUENDO, Evidon, Seers, and open-source equivalents (Spatie Cookie Consent, c15t, Yett). The category exhibits a structural irony: tools designed to implement privacy law themselves operate as third-party scripts that observe every page-view, capture consent-interaction telemetry, and frequently set their own cookies. Multiple academic and regulatory studies have documented dark patterns (pre-ticked boxes, asymmetric Accept/Reject buttons, hidden 'Reject all') across major CMPs, and France's CNIL fined Google EUR 150M in 2022 over such patterns.
67.name: Cookie compliance
67.summary_privacy: The CMP paradox: tools implementing consent themselves collect behavioural data (consent interactions, IP, fingerprint). Regulator action concrete: NoYB filed 226 GDPR complaints (Aug 2024) vs OneTrust banners with deceptive defaults; CA CPPA fined Honda USD 632,500 (Mar 2025) for asymmetric opt-in; CNIL fined Google EUR 150M (2022). EDPB Opinion 08/2024 rejects consent-or-pay. Many CMPs feed IAB TCF v2 to ad partners. Treat as third-party processor; require DPA; verify opt-out default.
67.summary_security: CMPs are first-loading client-side scripts that gate every downstream tag - a compromised CMP is an XSS/skimmer foothold across thousands of sites. Supply-chain risk is significant (single CDN script across the customer base); ~80% of CMP cookies are XSS-susceptible per recent research. CVE history exists (auth bypass, IDOR exposing cookie inventories). SOC2 attestations are common. The script also creates an availability SPOF when banner load blocks the page.

68.description: Accessibility tooling encompasses automated WCAG/ADA scanners, audit dashboards (Silktide, UsableNet, Allyant, Texthelp, Monsido), and the controversial 'accessibility overlay' widgets (AccessiBe, UserWay, EqualWeb, Recite Me, AccessiWay, All in One Accessibility, AdaSiteCompliance). Overlays inject a third-party JS bundle that attempts to rewrite the DOM/ARIA on the fly and present a user-facing toolbar. The disability community, WebAIM, and most accessibility consultants oppose overlays - they rarely fix underlying issues and have been linked to lawsuits, user-tracking concerns, and security incidents (e.g. Imperva-disclosed DOM XSS in EqualWeb affecting Fiverr, Bosch, Playtika, Avis; FTC fined accessiBe USD 1M in 2025 for deceptive marketing).
68.name: Accessibility
68.summary_privacy: Overlay widgets ask users to self-identify disabilities (vision, cognitive, motor, seizure-prone) and probe for assistive-tech - health-adjacent special-category data under GDPR Art 9 / CCPA sensitive PI. WebAIM and the disability community raise "digital redlining" concerns. Overlays often load analytics pixels. The European Commission has stated overlays do not deliver EAA compliance. Treat overlays as high-privacy processors needing explicit consent.
68.summary_security: Overlay vendors inject third-party JS that reads and rewrites the DOM, opening an XSS-class supply-chain vector across every customer site. Imperva disclosed DOM XSS in EqualWeb (CVE-2022-42960) affecting Fiverr, Bosch, Playtika, Avis, Zara, YOTPO, IronSource. The widget CDN, build pipeline, and admin console are high-value targets; compromise propagates everywhere. FTC USD 1M settlement vs accessiBe (final order Apr 2025) signals scrutiny. Audit-only tools (Silktide, UsableNet) far lower risk.

69.description: Authentication encompasses the systems that establish who a user is: passwordless and WebAuthn providers (Passage/1Password, WWPass), social/federated logins (Apple Sign-in, Login with Amazon, Microsoft Auth, LINE Login), CIAM platforms (Azure AD B2C, Amazon Cognito, LoginRadius, JumpCloud, Auth0), identity-verification/KYC (Onfido, Socure, MetaMap), 2FA services (Authy/Twilio), and OSS frameworks (NextAuth.js, Apereo CAS, Userbase, Alliance Auth). It is the highest-stakes category in this batch: identity is now the primary attack surface, and a single compromised IdP exposes every downstream relying party. The category sees the most concentrated PII (email, phone, government IDs in KYC flows, biometric templates), the highest regulatory scrutiny (GDPR, CCPA, SOC2, plus sector overlays like HIPAA, GLBA, PCI-DSS for payment-bound auth), and persistent attacker focus (OAuth token theft, SSO bypass, MFA fatigue, federation misconfiguration, IDP supply-chain incidents like Okta 2023).
69.name: Authentication
69.summary_privacy: Auth platforms hold the densest PII on the web: email, phone, government IDs (KYC), biometric templates (Onfido, Socure, MetaMap, Vouched), device fingerprints, login geolocation, and user-to-site relationship graphs (federated login lets the IdP see every relying party visited). Apple Sign-in uniquely offers privacy-preserving relays. Login with Amazon/LINE/Microsoft leak audience. KYC vendors process Art 9 data. DPAs, data residency, sub-processor lists demand scrutiny.
69.summary_security: Identity is the primary attack surface; one IdP compromise cascades to every federated service. Recent: Okta 2023 (HAR session tokens stolen, BeyondTrust + Cloudflare hijacked); Snowflake 2024 (UNC5537 abused stolen creds vs 165 tenants lacking MFA); Cisco Duo Apr 2024 (MFA SMS logs via telephony supplier); MS Midnight Blizzard Jan 2024 (SVR password-spray + legacy OAuth). Recurrent: OAuth/SAML flaws, JWT alg=none, MFA fatigue, SIM-swap, XSS token theft. FIDO2/WebAuthn baseline.

70.description: SSL/TLS certificate authorities (CAs) issue the digital certificates that bind public keys to verified identities, enabling HTTPS and authenticated TLS sessions across the web. The category spans free, automated DV-only authorities such as Let's Encrypt (operated by the nonprofit ISRG), commercial CAs such as DigiCert, Sectigo, GlobalSign, Entrust, and IdenTrust, and cloud-managed services like AWS Certificate Manager. CAs sit at the root of internet trust: every browser, OS, and TLS client ships a trust store of root CA certificates, and any single CA can technically issue a certificate for any domain. Modern operations are governed by the CA/Browser Forum Baseline Requirements, Mozilla and Microsoft root program policies, mandatory Certificate Transparency (CT) log submission, and automation via ACME. Although CAs themselves are infrastructure (server-side issuance and revocation, OCSP/CRL distribution) and rarely embed user-tracking code, mis-issuance incidents (DigiNotar 2011, Symantec 2017 distrust, TrustCor 2022, Entrust distrusted by Chrome in 2024) demonstrate that CA failures have systemic, internet-wide consequences.
70.name: SSL/TLS certificate authorities
70.summary_privacy: PII exposure is low: CAs see domain names, requester contact and validation evidence, not browsing activity. However CT logs deliberately publish every issued FQDN, so internal naming routinely leaks (a known recon source); wildcards or CT redaction mitigate. OCSP historically exposed which site a user visited to the CA; Let's Encrypt ended OCSP Aug 2025 (replaced with CRLs) citing this privacy risk. No client-side tracker behaviour.
70.summary_security: CAs are internet-scale trust anchors: a non-compliant CA can mis-issue for any domain, enabling MITM at huge scale (DigiNotar 2011; Symantec 2017; Entrust distrusted by Chrome/Mozilla/Apple Nov 2024 after 22+ incidents and 26k EV mis-issuances; DigiCert Jul 2024 revoked 83,267 certs over a CNAME-prefix DV defect). Risk concentrates in issuance pipelines, ACME/API keys, key custody, DV and CT compliance. Prefer CAs with strong WebTrust audits, public IR, automation and short-lived certs; use CAA.

71.description: Affiliate program platforms and networks (impact.com, ShareASale, Awin, CJ Affiliate, Rakuten Advertising, Post Affiliate Pro, Tapfiliate, PartnerStack, Refersion, LeadDyno, AccessTrade, etc.) connect advertisers with publishers, influencers, and partners to drive performance-based marketing. They operate through client-side tracking scripts, conversion pixels, redirect/click-tracking domains, and cookies (typically 30–90 day attribution windows) to attribute sales and pay commissions. Most networks act as third parties on the publisher's site and as data processors on the advertiser's site. Tracking relies heavily on third-party cookies, fingerprinting fallbacks, server-to-server postbacks, and cross-domain identifiers; tracker density is consistently high. Under GDPR/ePrivacy, affiliate cookies almost always require prior opt-in consent because they are not strictly necessary, and a Data Processing Agreement is required with every affiliate. CNIL and other regulators have actively fined advertisers for non-consented affiliate pixels.
71.name: Affiliate programs
71.summary_privacy: Critical: affiliate platforms are textbook third-party trackers — persistent cookies, cross-site click trails, many-to-many ID sharing with advertisers/publishers, and S2S forwarding of order/customer data. GDPR/ePrivacy require prior consent (not strictly necessary except user-requested cashback/loyalty) and a DPA per network. Awin uses legitimate-interest — a contested EU position. Payloads include order value, hashed email, customer ID, IP — often US-transferred. Heavy CCPA/CPRA "sale/share".
71.summary_security: Affiliate networks add third-party JS, redirect chains and pixels that expand the supply-chain attack surface (script injection, rogue creatives, cookie stuffing, click fraud — prosecuted as US wire fraud 18 USC 1343). Attribution servers hold order value, customer IDs and email hashes — skimming targets. Smaller networks lack script governance; SRI and CMP gating rare. S2S postbacks (Impact, CJ, Awin, Rakuten) cut client risk but extend trust to backends. Treat tags as untrusted code.

72.description: Appointment scheduling platforms (Calendly, SavvyCal, Doodle, Acuity, Microsoft Bookings, Cal.com, Mews, HotDoc, Doctena, Skedify, Mangomint, Glofox, Squire, Zenoti, Kalix, etc.) let visitors book time slots with a person or business, typically embedded as a widget or hosted scheduling page. They collect booker identity data (name, email, phone), reason-for-meeting notes, calendar availability, time zone/IP, and frequently integrate with CRMs, video conferencing (Zoom, Teams, Meet), email/SMS providers, payment processors, and analytics. Healthcare- and finance-oriented variants handle especially sensitive PII and may need HIPAA/BAA (Kalix, HotDoc, CoconutSoftware) or financial-services controls. Most are hosted SaaS on US infrastructure, increasing cross-border transfer obligations under GDPR/Schrems II. Calendly explicitly relies on the EU-US Data Privacy Framework and SCCs. Tracking density is usually light but not negligible because many embed marketing analytics on the booking page.
72.name: Appointment scheduling
72.summary_privacy: PII-heavy: every booking captures contact details plus free-text health, legal or business context. Vendor is processor; controllers need DPA, lawful basis, retention limits and consent for recording. Most vendors US-hosted (SCCs/DPF for EU). HIPAA varies: Calendly refuses BAAs and forbids PHI; Acuity (Squarespace) signs BAAs only on Premium/Powerhouse and only Acuity is in-scope; Cal.com offers self-hosted and signed BAA on enterprise. Calendar metadata is over-retained.
72.summary_security: Scheduling brokers calendar/email/video/CRM/payment integrations, so each connector enlarges attack surface (OAuth theft, webhook spoofing, misconfig). Booking links are heavily abused for impersonation phishing: an ongoing Calendly-themed AiTM campaign (2024-25) spoofs 75+ brands and steals Google/M365/Meta sessions, sometimes via Browser-in-the-Browser bypassing MFA. Public booking pages enumerate staff/email patterns. Require SOC 2 II, MFA, scoped OAuth, audit logs, DMARC.

73.description: Survey and feedback platforms (SurveyMonkey, Typeform, Qualtrics, Google Forms, Jotform, Survicate, Delighted, InMoment, Hotjar Surveys, Refiner, Iterate, etc.) collect structured responses, NPS/CSAT/CES scores, free-text feedback, and behavioural data through web embeds, intercept pop-ups, email links, and mobile SDKs. They are processors for response data and controllers for service-usage telemetry. Typical data items include name, email, demographic answers, opinions, and sometimes health, financial, or HR information depending on use case. Notable risk events include the 2018 Typeform breach (tens of thousands of customers' response data exfiltrated). Major vendors hold SOC 2 and ISO 27001 (SurveyMonkey ISO 27001 since 2019, SOC 2 since 2021); some offer HIPAA-eligible plans. Most store EU data in the US, relying on SCCs or the EU-US DPF, which remains under legal challenge.
73.name: Surveys
73.summary_privacy: High PII exposure by design: surveys often capture identifiable, sensitive or special-category data (health, demographics, opinions, employee feedback). Even "anonymous" surveys carry re-identifying free text plus IP/UA. Customers are controllers; vendor is processor — DPA required, EU data needs SCCs/DPF (mostly US-hosted). Embed pixels and CRM integrations can re-broadcast to ad-tech. Healthcare/education needs HIPAA/FERPA variants and minimum-necessary design. Public result links leak.
73.summary_security: Surveys aggregate large volumes of user content — high-value breach targets. Typeform's Jun 2018 unencrypted-backup theft exposed >100k records (Monzo ~20k, Revolut, Travelodge). Risks: public result links, weak ACLs, file-upload malware staging, embed XSS (CrowdSignal/Polldaddy CSRF/XSS incl. CVE-2024-43338), over-broad sharing. Embeds need CSP. Demand SOC 2 II, SSO/MFA, granular perms, encryption at rest. SurveyMonkey Enterprise/Qualtrics are HIPAA/ISO 27001; Typeform/Google Forms not.

74.description: A/B testing and experimentation platforms (Optimizely, VWO, AB Tasty, Kameleoon, Convert, Google Optimize legacy, Statsig, LaunchDarkly experiments, Adobe Target, SiteSpect, ABLyft, Intellimize, Varify, Shoplift, etc.) split traffic between variants and measure conversion outcomes via client-side JavaScript "snippets", server-side SDKs, or edge workers. Client-side variants typically run a synchronous blocking script in the page head to avoid flicker, which has a long history of XSS-adjacent issues (Optimizely's visual editor and inline-execution of variant code). Platforms set cookies and/or localStorage to keep visitors in consistent variants and log events including URL, referrer, UA, IP, and outcome data. Most operate as third-party data processors and require GDPR/ePrivacy consent before assignment because the cookies are not strictly necessary; running experiments on opted-out visitors collects data without a legal basis. Modern alternatives push toward server-side and consent-gated experimentation.
74.name: A/B Testing
74.summary_privacy: A/B cookies are NOT strictly necessary under GDPR/ePrivacy — CNIL/ICO guidance names VWO, Convert, Optimizely, AB Tasty and Adobe Target as requiring prior opt-in. Running tests on opted-out users is a recurring enforcement finding; CNIL imposed >EUR 139M in cookie-banner fines 2022-24 (11 in 2024). Common failure: variants applied before CMP init. Events include URL, referrer, viewport, UA, IP — re-identifying. Integrations broadcast exposure to ad-tech. US-hosted (SCCs/DPF).
74.summary_security: Client-side A/B platforms inject page-rewriting JS with broad DOM privileges — a high-impact supply-chain target. A compromised snippet can rewrite forms, exfiltrate inputs or stage skimming; visual editors allow arbitrary HTML/JS outside engineering review. Server-side/edge experimentation (GrowthBook, Split, LaunchDarkly, Optimizely Full Stack) shifts risk to flag misconfig, identity-graph leakage and SDK supply chain. Demand SOC 2 II, SSO/MFA, change audit, RBAC, CSP-compatible loading.

75.description: Email marketing, transactional, and bulk-send platforms (Mailchimp, SendGrid/Twilio, Mailgun, Postmark, Klaviyo, ActiveCampaign, Brevo/Sendinblue, Constant Contact, HubSpot Email, Bento, Bronto, Delivra, Robly, e-goi, INBOX, SmartEmailing, etc.) store subscriber lists, send campaigns and transactional messages, render templates, and track opens/clicks via tracking pixels and rewritten URLs. They are processors for subscriber data and often controllers for usage telemetry. The category sits at the intersection of CAN-SPAM, CASL, GDPR (Art. 6 lawful basis, ePrivacy direct-marketing consent), and CCPA. Mailchimp, Mailgun, and SendGrid suffered the 2022 mobile-API-key leak affecting ~54M users; Mailchimp had multiple social-engineering breaches in 2022–2023 affecting customer audiences and crypto-related lists; Twilio (SendGrid's parent) was breached in 2022. Most major vendors hold SOC 2 and ISO 27001 and rely on the EU-US DPF plus SCCs for transfers — still legally contested in the EU.
75.name: Email
75.summary_privacy: Email platforms process large volumes of identifiable PII — addresses, names, segmentation, behavioural and purchase data — and power identity resolution into the marketing stack. GDPR demands lawful basis (consent/soft opt-in), suppression portability, DPA; CAN-SPAM/CASL require unsubscribe. Open/click pixels are cookie-equivalent under ePrivacy and increasingly need EU consent; some DPAs treat pre-load pixels as unlawful. EU data mostly US-hosted (SCCs/DPF). Avoid special-category PII.
75.summary_security: ESPs hold large, high-value subscriber datasets and are repeatedly breached. Mailchimp suffered three support-tool social-engineering attacks in <12 months: Apr 2022 (Trezor), Aug 2022 (DigitalOcean), Jan 2023 (WooCommerce, Statista, FanDuel). Twilio's Aug 2022 phishing also hit SendGrid. A 2022 Salt Labs leak exposed mobile-API keys across Mailchimp/Mailgun/SendGrid (~54M). Apr 2025 "Satanic" 849k SendGrid claim was disputed. Require SOC 2 II, ISO 27001, scoped API keys, SSO/MFA, DKIM/DMARC.

76.description: Website and app personalisation platforms (Dynamic Yield, Monetate, Adobe Target, Salesforce Personalization/Interaction Studio, Acquia Personalization/Lift, Bloomreach, Mutiny, Kibo Personalization, Intellimize, Karte, Kameleoon, Frosmo, Clerk.io, LimeSpot, Fresh Relevance, Attraqt, Breinify, Connectif, Loop54, Recombee, Epoq, Target2Sell, Linx Impulse, etc.) tailor content, product recommendations, banners, search results, and offers in real time based on visitor identifiers, behavioural history, segments, and ML models. They typically deploy a client-side script with high DOM privileges and stream event data to a vendor-hosted decisioning engine that also ingests CRM/CDP data. Cookies and device IDs persist user-level profiles across sessions and often across domains. Data flows resemble ad-tech: third-party identifiers, cross-property profile enrichment, and lookalike modelling. Under GDPR these activities almost always require consent (profiling, automated decisioning), Art. 22 disclosures, and granular opt-out; CCPA treats most personalisation data flows as a "sale/share" requiring opt-out signals (GPC).
76.name: Personalisation
76.summary_privacy: Profiling and automated content selection make this among the most privacy-sensitive client-side categories. Persistent IDs combine on-site behaviour with CRM, purchase and third-party data into long-lived profiles, raising GDPR Art. 6/9/22 — pricing, eligibility and ranking can be "solely automated decisions with similarly significant effects" requiring explicit consent, human review and contestability. CCPA/CPRA "sharing" and GPC required when IDs leave the controller. SCCs/DPF for EU/US.
76.summary_security: Personalisation scripts inject content into the DOM with broad privileges and load decisions from vendor edge servers — a compromise can rewrite pages, prices and forms (Magecart blast radius). Many ingest CRM/CDP data, extending reach to first-party customer records. Visual editors and merchandiser rules bypass engineering review. Vendor ML can leak signal across tenants if isolation is weak. Require SOC 2 II, SSO/MFA, change audit, CSP-friendly loaders, scoped API keys, CDP egress controls.

77.description: Retargeting (a.k.a. behavioural retargeting or remarketing) technologies track visitors who have interacted with a site and serve them personalised display, social, native, or programmatic ads on other properties to bring them back to convert. Implementations rely on a client-side pixel/SDK that drops a cookie or local identifier, exchanges hashed user data, and broadcasts events (page view, add-to-cart, purchase) to one or more demand-side platforms, supply-side platforms, and data brokers via real-time bidding (RTB). Audience lists are often enriched with third-party identity graphs and synced across multiple ad networks, making this category one of the most privacy-invasive client-side technology classes on the open web. Representative vendors in the catalog include Criteo, AdRoll, Yahoo Advertising, RTB House, MNTN, Captify, OptiMonk and many niche regional players. Under GDPR / ePrivacy and the U.S. state privacy laws (CCPA/CPRA, VCDPA, etc.), retargeting pixels are non-essential trackers requiring prior, opt-in, granular consent; misuse has produced some of the largest adtech enforcement actions to date, most notably the EUR 40 million CNIL fine against Criteo confirmed by the Conseil d'Etat.
77.name: Retargeting
77.summary_privacy: Highest-risk privacy category: persistent cross-site tracking, identity-graph syncing and RTB broadcast. ePrivacy Art. 5(3) and EDPB Guidelines 2/2023 require prior opt-in consent; CCPA/CPRA require opt-out of sale/share. Conseil d'Etat upheld the CNIL EUR 40M Criteo fine on 4 March 2026, confirming hashed IDs are personal data. US healthcare Meta Pixel litigation exceeds USD 100M (Advocate Aurora 12.25M, Mass General Brigham 18.4M, Christ Hospital 7M, GoodRx FTC, BetterHelp FTC).
77.summary_security: Third-party JavaScript pixels with broad DOM and form access; the RTB partner sprawl behind each retargeting tag (DSPs, SSPs, identity-graph vendors, data brokers) materially widens the supply-chain attack surface. Documented incidents include the Picreel mass skimmer, Magecart pivots through tag-manager misconfiguration, and silent exfiltration of form data. Enforce CSP, SRI, per-vendor tag governance, server-side tagging where possible, and inventory the long tail of downstream RTB recipients.

78.description: Real User Monitoring (RUM) tools instrument production websites and apps with a client-side JavaScript SDK that captures per-session performance telemetry from actual end-user devices: Core Web Vitals (LCP, CLS, INP), navigation timing, resource timing, JS errors, AJAX/XHR/fetch latencies, route changes, console logs and, increasingly, full session-replay recordings of the DOM. Telemetry is shipped to a vendor-hosted ingestion endpoint (or self-hosted backend for OSS variants) and stitched into sessions via first-party cookies. Representative vendors in the catalog include New Relic Browser, Datadog RUM, Splunk RUM, Dynatrace RUM, Microsoft Application Insights, AppDynamics, Akamai mPulse, Amazon CloudWatch RUM, Cloudflare Browser Insights, Sentry, Raygun, SpeedCurve, Pingdom RUM, Instana, Elastic APM, Boomerang, and the OSS web-vitals library. While the primary purpose is observability rather than advertising, URL paths, query strings, click targets, form interactions and session replays routinely contain personal data, making RUM materially in-scope for GDPR/CCPA when masking and consent are not configured properly.
78.name: RUM
78.summary_privacy: Captures URLs (often containing IDs/tokens/emails), IP, UA, geolocation and, with session replay, DOM and keystrokes. Vendor docs (Datadog, Splunk, Raygun) place masking/consent burden on the customer; defaults leak. EDPB Guidelines 2/2023 (Oct 2024) bring URL tracking into ePrivacy Art. 5(3). US CIPA/wiretap class actions over session replay accelerated in 2025 (9th Cir. Mikulsky v. Bloomingdale's, June 2025). Mask PII, gate replay behind consent, sign DPAs.
78.summary_security: Third-party JS SDKs with broad DOM/fetch/error and (with session replay) pixel-level capture; SDK or CDN compromise is equivalent to same-origin XSS on every instrumented page. Session-replay expands blast radius dramatically and requires strict masking/allow-deny lists. Pin SDK versions, enforce SRI and CSP, prefer self-hosted or vendor-pinned bundles, audit ingestion endpoints. Treat as high-privilege tag governance just like marketing pixels.

79.description: Geolocation technologies map a visitor's IP address (or, less commonly, the HTML5 Geolocation API / GPS coordinates) to a country, region, city, ASN, time-zone, and proxy/VPN/Tor classification. They are used for content localisation, currency/language switching, fraud and abuse scoring, geo-blocking for export-control / licensing, marketing analytics, and security (e.g. impossible-travel detection). The category covers commercial geo-IP databases and REST APIs such as MaxMind GeoIP, IPinfo, ipstack, ip-api, ipapi, ipify, ipdata, ipgeolocation, ipbase, db-ip, IPInfoDB, IP2Location, BigDataCloud, and platform offerings like Microsoft Azure Maps, the Shopify Geolocation App, store-locator products (Bullseye, StoreRocket), and consent-aware solutions like Radar and Geo Targetly. Server-side lookups against a local database are the lowest-risk pattern; client-side calls to a third-party JSON endpoint expose the visitor IP and Referer to that vendor and create a tracker. The HTML5 Geolocation API additionally exposes precise lat/long which is unambiguously personal data under GDPR.
79.name: Geolocation
79.summary_privacy: IP addresses are personal data under GDPR; combining IP with city/lat-long builds a trackable profile. EDPB Guidelines 2/2023 (Oct 2024) explicitly cover "tracking based on IP only" under ePrivacy Art. 5(3). Client-side calls to third-party geo APIs share visitor IP and Referer with the vendor and count as a tracker; server-side lookups against a local MaxMind/db-ip DB are the preferred pattern. MaxMind's July 2025 DPA forbids identifying households or individuals.
79.summary_security: Offline DB lookups (MaxMind, db-ip, IP2Location) have minimal direct attack surface. Client-side fetch to a third-party JSON endpoint (ipapi.co, ip-api.com, ipinfo, ipgeolocation) is a remote dependency that, if compromised or DNS-hijacked, pivots to broader script injection: pin endpoints, enforce TLS, do not pass unrelated PII as query parameters. HTML5 Geolocation requires user permission and exposes precise lat/long. Treat any geo-IP key in client JS as low-secret.

80.description: WordPress themes are PHP/HTML/CSS/JS template packages that render the front-end of a WordPress site and frequently bundle their own page builders, widgets, custom post types, font loaders, and integration scripts (Google Fonts, jQuery libraries, hosted icon sets, marketing tags). Because they run in the same PHP process as core WordPress and execute on every request, a vulnerable theme can lead to stored XSS, arbitrary file upload, SQL injection, privilege escalation, or full RCE on the host. Wordfence Intelligence and Patchstack track tens of thousands of WordPress ecosystem CVEs across plugins and themes (241 new vulnerabilities in a recent weekly report; ~64,000 total tracked as of 2026), and "nulled" (pirated) premium themes are a common vector for hidden backdoors, base64-obfuscated PHP malware, hidden admin users, SEO spam and traffic redirects. Catalog entries cover both reputable free/commercial themes (AndersNoren, GoDaddy Primer, Hestia, Kadence Virtue, Catch Themes, Cryout Creations) and small one-off niche products. Front-end tracker exposure is generally moderate; security risk is the dominant concern.
80.name: WordPress themes
80.summary_privacy: Themes themselves rarely set tracking cookies, but commonly load remote assets (Google Fonts, Gravatar, CDN icon sets, embedded YouTube/Maps) that leak visitor IP/UA to third parties and require cookie-consent gating under GDPR/ePrivacy and the EDPB's 2024 Art. 5(3) guidelines. Page-builder starter demos sometimes import marketing pixels (Meta, GA) by default; audit the active theme's outbound requests before relying on the consent banner.
80.summary_security: Themes execute server-side PHP with full WordPress privileges and ship their own JS/CSS; common CVE classes are XSS, CSRF, file upload, SQLi, IDOR and PHP object injection. Patchstack 2025 mid-year disclosed 4,462 ecosystem CVEs (themes ~4-9%); Sucuri 2025 research shows >30% of nulled themes ship with hidden file managers, wp-vcd admin backdoors, base64 PHP shells, or SEO-spam injectors. Use only WordPress.org or vendor-signed builds, patch promptly, scan with Wordfence/Patchstack.

81.description: Shopify themes are Liquid-templated storefronts (HTML/CSS/JS + Liquid + theme settings) that render on Shopify's hosted, multi-tenant infrastructure. Unlike WordPress, themes do not execute arbitrary server-side code: rendering happens inside Shopify's sandboxed Liquid engine, drastically reducing classic RCE/SQLi exposure. Risk is concentrated on the client side: Liquid output that does not properly escape user input can introduce DOM XSS; theme-bundled JS, third-party app embeds, trust-badge widgets, and review/conversion apps can fingerprint visitors, leak PII to ad networks, or be hijacked via supply-chain compromise of the loaded JS. Theme apps and "conversion booster" packs (e.g. Conversion Bear, Shoptimized in the catalog, plus mainstream themes such as Belliza) request OAuth scopes (read_customers, write_themes) that should be carefully reviewed. Because the storefront handles payment flows, Shopify themes also intersect PCI-DSS scope when a theme renders custom checkout or cart fields.
81.name: Shopify themes
81.summary_privacy: Storefront themes routinely embed Meta Pixel, Google Ads, TikTok Pixel, plus reviews and chat widgets that share cart and customer data with third parties; EDPB Guidelines 2/2023 (final Oct 2024) bring all such pixels under ePrivacy Art. 5(3). EU storefronts must surface a compliant cookie/consent banner; checkout and customer-account flows handle PII subject to GDPR/CCPA, and custom checkout extensions can pull the theme into PCI-DSS scope.
81.summary_security: Shopify's hosted Liquid runtime eliminates most server-side RCE/SQLi risk, but client-side XSS via unescaped Liquid output, malicious third-party app scripts, and over-broad OAuth scopes (write_themes, read_customers) remain the dominant CVE classes - e.g. CVE-2025-55998 (Smart Search & Filter App XSS) and HackerOne XSS reports in *.myshopify.com. Magecart skimmers target storefronts via compromised app JS. Enforce CSP, scope-minimise theme apps, use Shopify Store Health.

82.description: Drupal themes are Twig-templated front-end packages running inside Drupal's PHP application. Since Drupal 8 the theming layer uses Twig (replacing the legacy PHPTemplate engine) with auto-escaping, which materially reduced XSS exposure compared to early Drupal 6/7 themes. However, Twig itself has produced critical advisories in Drupal core: SA-CORE-2022-016 (multiple vulnerabilities including arbitrary code execution via render arrays in Twig), CVE-2022-39261 (arbitrary file read via crafted namespace path in the filesystem loader), and recurring guidance against the |raw filter and unquoted attribute output. Misuse of |raw, custom Twig extensions, and themes that allow user-supplied template fragments enable XSS, SSRF, or LFI. Because Drupal frequently powers government, university, and enterprise sites, themes also intersect strict regulatory regimes (GDPR, FERPA, public-sector accessibility). Front-end tracker density of stock Drupal themes is generally low; the dominant risk is security and the operator's own choice to embed marketing/analytics scripts.
82.name: Drupal themes
82.summary_privacy: Default themes set few cookies and load few third-party assets; privacy risk is dominated by operator-added analytics/marketing modules and embedded media (YouTube, Google Maps, Fonts) which still require cookie-consent gating under GDPR/ePrivacy and the EDPB's 2024 Art. 5(3) guidelines. Many Drupal sites are public-sector (.gov, .edu) which intersects FERPA and public-sector accessibility/transparency obligations.
82.summary_security: Twig auto-escaping mitigates most XSS, but misuse of |raw, unquoted attributes, custom Twig extensions, or render-array abuse leads to XSS, file read or RCE: SA-CORE-2022-016 (RCE via render arrays), CVE-2022-39261 (Twig namespace file read), CVE-2025-24374 (Twig null-coalesce gap, core not vulnerable), CVE-2025-3062 (XSS in Drupal Admin LTE). Restrict who can edit Twig templates, keep core and Twig patched, review contrib themes. Smaller attack surface than WordPress themes.

83.description: Browser fingerprinting derives a stable, cookie-less visitor identifier from a combination of passive browser/device attributes (User-Agent, screen geometry, time-zone, language, installed fonts, audio/canvas/WebGL rendering quirks, hardware concurrency, codec support, battery, gyroscope, etc.). Combining ~20 attributes yields uniqueness for over 95% of browsers (EFF Panopticlick research) and persists across incognito mode, cookie clearing, and most consent rejections, making it the canonical "stealth" or "post-cookie" tracking technique. Use cases split sharply: legitimate fraud prevention and bot defence (ThreatMetrix / LexisNexis Risk Solutions, TransUnion TruValidate / iovation, MaxMind minFraud, FingerprintJS Pro's Smart Signals and BotD library, ClientJS) versus covert cross-site advertising tracking that bypasses cookie banners. Under GDPR, ePrivacy, and the French CNIL's storage-and-access guidance, fingerprinting is treated equivalently to cookies and requires prior, freely-given consent for non-essential purposes; CNIL has fined adtech vendors for fingerprinting without consent. Academic studies (ACM WWW 2021 "User Tracking in the Post-cookie Era") document widespread bypass of consent through fingerprinting.
83.name: Browser fingerprinting
83.summary_privacy: Highest-risk: stable cross-site, cross-incognito identifier that persists across cookie clearing and consent rejection. EDPB Guidelines 2/2023 (16 Oct 2024) treat fingerprinting equivalently to cookies under ePrivacy Art. 5(3); non-fraud uses require prior opt-in consent. EFF: fingerprinting on ~40% of Tranco top-10k. Google's 16 Feb 2025 reversal allowing fingerprinting for advertisers was called "irresponsible" by the UK ICO. CNIL has fined adtech vendors for fingerprinting without consent.
83.summary_security: Defensive use cases (anti-fraud, ATO defence, bot mitigation by FingerprintJS Pro, ThreatMetrix, TruValidate/iovation, MaxMind minFraud, Sift, Forter) deliver real security value. Risk: the SDK reads high-entropy browser surfaces (canvas, WebGL, audio, fonts, battery, hardware concurrency) and ships a stable identifier off-domain; SDK/CDN compromise yields broad client-side observability. Treat as high-privilege third-party JS: pin versions, enforce SRI/CSP, restrict to fraud-only paths.

84.description: Loyalty and rewards platforms manage points, tiers, referrals, gift cards, and cashback programs that brands embed in ecommerce and retail apps to drive repeat purchases. Vendors such as Yotpo Loyalty, Smile.io, LoyaltyLion, Antavo, Marsello, Extole, Talkable, and Kangaroo Rewards collect customer identifiers (email, phone, name, purchase history, device IDs) and exchange them with Shopify, Klaviyo, Salesforce, and POS systems via APIs, webhooks, and embedded JavaScript widgets. Programs frequently combine first-party CRM data with marketing automation, behavioral segmentation, and social-referral tracking, which means a single loyalty integration becomes a long-lived processor of consumer PII and transaction data subject to GDPR, CCPA, and PCI-adjacent obligations when payment and refund logic is exposed. Open-source point engines like MyCred are minority players; the category is overwhelmingly commercial SaaS.
84.name: Loyalty & rewards
84.summary_privacy: Loyalty programs build identity-bound profiles: email, SMS, phone, name, address, birthdate, lifetime purchase history, referral graph. They sync audiences to ad networks (Meta, Klaviyo, Google) and drop referral/conversion cookies, so GDPR Art. 6/7 consent, ePrivacy cookie consent, and CCPA sale/share apply. EU regulators have flagged referral cookies; require DPAs, sub-processor disclosure, EU residency, GPC/TCF propagation.
84.summary_security: Loyalty widgets load in authenticated commerce flows with broad OAuth scopes for orders, customers, refunds, and discounts (Shopify/Magento/BigCommerce). Checkout-adjacent third-party JS is a Magecart skimming surface; Smile.io, LoyaltyLion, Yotpo, Antavo, Extole, ReferralCandy hold multi-tenant PII whose breach hits every merchant. Verify SOC2 Type 2, scoped rotatable app tokens, webhook HMAC signing, CSP isolation of widget origins, admin SSO+MFA.

85.description: Feature management platforms (LaunchDarkly, Split, Statsig, Flagsmith, Unleash, ConfigCat, AWS AppConfig) provide feature flags, remote configuration, gradual rollouts, kill switches, and A/B experimentation. Client and server SDKs evaluate flag rules against a user context (user ID, email, IP, country, custom attributes) that is sent to the vendor for targeting and exposure analytics. The same context powers experimentation pipelines, so each evaluation effectively becomes a behavioral event tied to a stable identifier. Customers also include LaunchNotes, Beamer, Featurebase, and Noticeable for product update/feedback workflows. Open-source options (Flagsmith, Unleash, OpenFeature) can be self-hosted, but the dominant deployment is SaaS with edge evaluation. The category sits in the request path of production traffic, making availability and integrity a security concern in addition to the privacy footprint of targeting attributes.
85.name: Feature management
85.summary_privacy: Targeting contexts include user IDs, email, IP, geolocation, device fingerprint, plan, and custom attributes — giving vendors behavioural telemetry usable for re-identification. GDPR Art. 28 DPA, sub-processor disclosure, EU residency, and user-key pseudonymisation are the primary controls; CCPA service-provider language applies. Check experiment retention before forwarding PII as flag context and whether the vendor uses data for model training (Statsig/OpenAI).
85.summary_security: Flag SDKs (LaunchDarkly, Statsig, Split, Flagsmith, Unleash, GrowthBook, ConfigCat) run in production with privileged config that can toggle security controls (MFA, geofencing, kill-switches) or dark-launch backdoors. A leaked SDK/env key is a direct production incident; rule contents leak business logic. Require regional failover, signed bootstrap, SOC2 Type 2, env isolation, RBAC+SSO, webhook signing, least-privilege keys. Statsig's 2025 OpenAI acquisition adds vendor-stability diligence.

86.description: Audience segmentation platforms ingest first-party and third-party behavioral data to build user/cohort profiles for advertising, personalization, and analytics. Examples include Adobe Audience Manager, Salesforce Audience Studio / Interaction Studio, Tealium AudienceStream, Oracle BlueKai, Cxense, Sirdata, Omeda, Fixel, Bloom Labs, and Viafoura. The category overlaps with Data Management Platforms (DMPs) and Customer Data Platforms (CDPs) and historically depends on cross-site cookies, mobile ad IDs, device fingerprints, and partner audience marketplaces. Segments are then activated into Google Ads, Meta, DSPs, and email automation. Because the explicit goal is to enrich identity and predict behavior across properties, segmentation tools have among the highest tracker densities and third-party data-sharing risk in the entire ecosystem, with Oracle BlueKai having been a notable breach disclosure case.
86.name: Segmentation
86.summary_privacy: Segmentation is the textbook target of GDPR Art. 4 profiling, Art. 22 ADM, CCPA sale/share, and ePrivacy cookie consent. Vendors enrich profiles via third-party marketplaces, run cross-context behavioural advertising, and transfer EU data to the US. TCF v2.2 and GPC must propagate into activations; without consent gating and EU residency, deployment is high regulatory risk. Reference: Schrems II, CNIL Criteo EUR40M, ICO ad-tech.
86.summary_security: DMP/segmentation tags read cookies, localStorage, and DOM, so a compromised tag is a cross-site exfiltration vector — the failure mode of the 2020 Oracle BlueKai 4TB exposure and recurring audience-manager supply-chain audits. Server-side activation APIs fan customer lists to dozens of ad partners, multiplying skimming and credential-stuffing surface. Require TMS loading with strict CSP, partner allowlists, signed payloads, scoped keys, full DPA chains, runtime tag integrity.

87.description: WordPress plugins are PHP extensions installed into a WordPress site, executing inside the web application with full database, filesystem, and HTTP-request privileges. The ecosystem spans tens of thousands of plugins covering page builders (Elementor, Oxygen, LiveCanvas), forms, ecommerce add-ons (WooCommerce extensions, Stripe gateways), caching, embeds, galleries, multilingual, performance, and integrations. WordPress powers roughly 40% of the public web, and plugins are the single largest source of disclosed web-application vulnerabilities tracked by Wordfence, Patchstack, and the WPScan database — weekly bulletins typically include dozens of new CVEs including critical RCE, SQLi, auth-bypass, and unauthenticated arbitrary file upload. Many plugins are abandoned or sold to opaque maintainers who introduce backdoors. The category covers everything from minor widgets to plugins handling payments, PII, and admin authentication.
87.name: WordPress plugins
87.summary_privacy: Plugins collect PII via forms, comments, checkouts, reviews, analytics. Many silently send admin telemetry and load third-party CDNs (Google Fonts, gravatars) — repeatedly cited in EU enforcement against the embedding site (LG München Google Fonts 2022, CNIL Meta Pixel 2022-2024). Payment, review, chat, tracking, translation plugins create undisclosed cross-border transfers. Maintain plugin inventory, enforce per-plugin DPAs, gate marketing plugins behind consent, audit pixels.
87.summary_security: The largest CVE class on the public web. Wordfence/Patchstack publish weekly criticals: unauth RCE, SQLi, privesc, arbitrary file upload across plugins with millions of installs. 2024 shipped CVE-2024-50550/44000 (LiteSpeed Cache), CVE-2024-25600 (Bricks RCE), CVE-2024-27956 (WP Automatic SQLi). Plugins run with full PHP/DB privileges; ownership transfers introduce malicious updates (Social Warfare, AccessPress). Require active maintenance, WAF, auto-updates, inventory, FIM.

88.description: Web hosting providers operate the physical or virtualized infrastructure (shared hosting, VPS, dedicated servers, managed WordPress, static/headless) that serves websites to end users. The category covers regional and specialist providers such as Infomaniak, Hostpoint, Mittwald, ALL-INKL, UKFast, Nexcess, Acquia, Hostens, Sakura Internet, Niagahoster, REG.RU, idCloudHost, Strattic, Elementor Cloud, and wp.cloud — distinct from hyperscale IaaS (AWS/GCP/Azure) which is tracked separately. Providers control DNS, mail routing, TLS termination, server patching, backups, and often shared control panels (cPanel, Plesk). Their security posture sets a floor for every site they host; misconfigurations or breaches at the hoster propagate to thousands of tenants, and shared hosting has historically been a high-volume vector for malware injection and credential theft.
88.name: Hosting
88.summary_privacy: Hosting providers are GDPR processors for all customer and visitor data on the platform, including server logs, mail, DB content, and backups. Data residency and EU-US transfers matter, especially for Swiss/EU vs US incumbents. Confirm DPA, sub-processor list (CDN, mail relay, backup, AV, anti-bot), region-locked storage, breach-notification SLAs, account-deletion. Admin-dashboard trackers and partner placements should be disclosed/opt-out. EU hosters default to better residency; verify.
88.summary_security: Hosting providers are critical processors: a breach exposes customer data, source, DBs, mail, TLS keys, and admin creds for every tenant. Shared/managed-WP hosts are repeatedly cited in mass-defacement and skimming due to weak isolation and outdated PHP/CPanel — GoDaddy 2021 Managed WP (1.2M creds) and 2022 multi-year intrusion, Hostinger 2019 (14M), OVHcloud SBG2 fire 2021, DreamHost 814M-record exposure, recurring Bluehost takeovers. Evaluate ISO 27001, SOC 2 Type 2, isolation, DDoS, MFA+SSO.

89.description: Website translation and localization tools render multilingual content via JavaScript widgets, server-side proxies, CMS plugins, or translation-management platforms. Examples include Google Translate widget, Weglot, GTranslate, ConveyThis, Linguise, Bablic, Easyling, Smartling, Crowdin, WPML, Polylang, Weblate, Transcy, and Shopify-specific apps like langify and LangShop. Client-side widgets typically inject a third-party script that POSTs page text or DOM segments to a translation backend and returns rendered HTML; some operate as full reverse proxies that re-serve the entire site from a vendor domain (SEO-friendly subdirectories or subdomains). Google Translate-derived widgets in particular load Google scripts that double as analytics/tracking endpoints. The category therefore mixes content infrastructure with potential third-party data flows whenever visitor URLs, headers, or page text are sent off-origin.
89.name: Translation
89.summary_privacy: Translation traffic includes URLs, referrers, IPs, full DOM text (names, addresses, order info, tickets), and cookies — sent to the vendor and, for Google Translate, into Google's processing chain. GDPR requires lawful basis, sub-processor disclosure, EU residency, and Schrems II TIAs. EU regulators flagged Google-hosted scripts as requiring prior consent (LG München Google Fonts; noyb GA). Confirm exclusion of authenticated/checkout pages and translation-memory retention.
89.summary_security: Client translation widgets are third-party scripts with full DOM access — CDN compromise yields XSS-equivalent control. Proxy-mode services (Weglot, Bablic, GTranslate, ConveyThis, Linguise, Smartling proxy) terminate TLS and rewrite every response, so the vendor sees URLs, headers, cookies, and form posts not excluded; key mgmt and IR SLAs become first-class controls. Google Translate pulls from Google domains that also serve tracking. Self-hosted WPML/Polylang/Weblate avoid that risk.

90.description: Reviews platforms collect, moderate, and display user-generated ratings, written reviews, photos, and video testimonials on ecommerce, service, and B2B sites. The category covers Trustpilot-style independent platforms, ecommerce-integrated reviews (Yotpo, Junip, Feefo, Reevoo, Ali Reviews, HulkApps), reputation/aggregation tools (Grade.us, Reviewshake, ReviewPro), and feedback/insights products (Alchemer Mobile, Appzi, Fider). Implementations combine post-purchase email/SMS request flows, on-site widgets/badges that load third-party JS, schema.org rich-result markup, and APIs that exchange order data with Shopify, Magento, BigCommerce, Klaviyo, and Google Shopping. Because they process customer PII, purchase history, and publicly displayed UGC, reviews systems sit at the intersection of marketing automation, content moderation, and consumer-protection regulation, with risk of fake-review enforcement (FTC) and GDPR right-to-erasure conflicts.
90.name: Reviews
90.summary_privacy: Reviews process buyer email, name, order/SKU, photos, video, IP — and publish portions publicly, creating durable, indexable, search-cached PII that conflicts with GDPR Art. 17 erasure and Art. 5(1)(e) storage limitation. Cross-merchant syndication (Yotpo, Trustpilot, Bazaarvoice Network) and Google Seller Ratings amplify sharing. Cookie consent applies to widgets/pixels. FTC 16 CFR Part 465 (2024) and EU Omnibus 2019/2161 require verification. Confirm DPA, retention, deletion SLAs.
90.summary_security: Reviews widgets run third-party JS on PDP/checkout-adjacent pages with DOM and cookie access; vendor-script compromise yields cross-merchant XSS and skimming (Magecart). UGC moderation and image/video uploads add server-side parsing risk (XXE, SSRF, ImageMagick CVEs, malware). Post-purchase email/SMS uses order-scoped tokens that, if leaked, allow buyer impersonation and fake-review injection. Verify webhook HMAC, token rotation, SRI/CSP, image scanning, moderator RBAC, fake-review controls.

91.description: Buy now pay later (BNPL) platforms (Klarna, Affirm, Afterpay/Block, Sezzle, Zip, PayPal Pay-in-4, Shop Pay Installments, plus regional variants SeQura, Aplazame, Mobicred, PayBright, KueskiPay, LayBuy, Payflex, Mokka, Postpay, ViaBill, Scalapay, Atome, Paidy) provide short-term, mostly interest-free instalment financing at checkout. Their tags load on the merchant site to render widgets, perform soft credit checks, score the shopper and complete the loan-application flow. Sitting on the checkout path they ingest full order detail, billing/shipping address, email, phone, device fingerprint, IP and a real-time identity-verification (KYC) packet, then share that financial PII with credit bureaus, fraud-prevention vendors, ad networks and analytics partners. Incogni (2024) and Consumer Reports (2023) found the average BNPL app collected 14 categories of personal data and shared up to 17 with third parties; Klarna, Afterpay and Block have all suffered material data incidents. The CFPB's May 2024 interpretive rule classifies pay-in-4 BNPL as credit cards under Regulation Z (TILA/CARD Act): periodic statements, dispute rights and billing-error procedures now apply. GDPR, CCPA/CPRA, PCI-DSS, GLBA Safeguards Rule and UK FCA consumer-credit rules also apply.
91.name: Buy now pay later
91.summary_privacy: Privacy-critical: BNPLs collect financial PII (name, DOB, address, phone, email, bank/card, income, fingerprint, precise location) and share with credit bureaus, fraud vendors, ad networks. Incogni 2024 found Afterpay shared 17 categories; Klarna and Affirm shipped precise-location to ad-tech. Soft credit pulls leave a bureau footprint without loan completion. CFPB May 2024 rule extends Reg Z to pay-in-4; GDPR, CPRA, GLBA Safeguards, ePrivacy apply. DSAR uneven.
91.summary_security: High-value surface: checkout JS plus loan/KYC APIs, with credit-bureau and fraud-vendor integrations multiplying blast radius. Klarna (2022) and Block/Afterpay (2022, 2024) breaches leaked records. Magecart-class risk on the widget; ATO, synthetic-identity fraud and coupon-stuffing on the loan API. PCI-DSS scope flows to the merchant; SOC 2/ISO 27001 uneven — regional BNPLs (Mokka, Postpay, Spotii, Payflex) publish far less than Klarna/Affirm/Afterpay.

92.description: Performance technologies cover web acceleration, image/video optimisation, edge caching, lazy-loading plugins, prefetch primitives and real-user monitoring (RUM). The bulk of the category is browser/CMS-side libraries and dev-tools with no tracker behaviour: LazySizes, Lozad.js, Quicklink, Intersection Observer, Priority Hints, Modern Image Formats, Turbo/Turbolinks, Partytown, Speculative Loading, Web Worker Offloading, EWWW Image Optimizer, ShortPixel, WP Fastest Cache, WP-Optimize, Autoptimize, Cloudflare Rocket Loader, Google PageSpeed. A smaller tier sits in-line with checkout as edge-proxy / RUM SDKs: Yottaa, Edgemesh, Fasterize, Speed Kit, NitroPack, Gumlet, ImageEngine, SpeedSize, Piio, Quicq, plus RUM vendors (DebugBear, Calibre, SpeedCurve, Pingdom, Catchpoint, ThousandEyes, Naver RUA, OneAPM, Quicksprout, Request Metrics, SpeedOf.Me, StatusCake, Cronitor). RUM tools inject a client-side beacon SDK that captures page-load timings, Core Web Vitals, JS errors, navigation paths, device/OS/browser, IP-derived geo and network conditions; edge-proxy vendors additionally terminate TLS and see full URLs, referrers and cookies. Most items have no consumer-PII path; the RUM/edge tier is explicitly in GDPR/ePrivacy scope.
92.name: Performance
92.summary_privacy: Most items are dev primitives with no tracker behaviour (40/71 sampled light or none, 0 heavy). The privacy-relevant tier is RUM: Core Web Vitals, click/scroll paths, JS error stacks, URLs, referrers, UA, device class and IP-derived geo are personal data under GDPR/ePrivacy and typically require consent. Tier-1 RUM (DebugBear, Calibre, SpeedCurve, Catchpoint) offers IP truncation and cookieless modes; the long tail (Naver RUA, OneAPM, Quicksprout, Website Speedy) is heavier.
92.summary_security: Two risk profiles. (1) Libraries / WP plugins / browser primitives (LazySizes, Lozad, Quicklink, Autoptimize, ShortPixel, EWWW, Partytown, Turbo) have minimal third-party surface — risk is maintainer/npm side. (2) Edge-proxy + RUM tier (Yottaa, Edgemesh, Fasterize, Speed Kit, NitroPack, DebugBear, Calibre, SpeedCurve, Catchpoint) sits inside the trusted execution context: a compromised SDK propagates to every page (Magecart-class). Edge-proxy vendors terminate TLS. Few publish SOC 2.

93.description: Reservations & delivery covers hospitality, restaurant, food-ordering and travel booking — restaurant table-reservation widgets (OpenTable, Resy, Tock, ResDiary, TableCheck, CoverManager, Formitable, Bookatable, Foratable, Guestonline, Tripleseat, Bottle), hotel/PMS and channel managers (Little Hotelier, Cubilis, Octorate, Eviivo, Clock PMS, Maxxton, LobbyPMS, Elina PMS, Revinate, Straiv, Guesty), short-term-rental platforms (BedBooking, HomHero, Booking Experts, Findigs), food-ordering and delivery (Lieferando, Olo, ChowNow, Menufy, Fleksa, Popmenu, Flipdish, DISH, EatStreet, BeyondMenu, Foodomaa, FoodBooking, GloriaFood) and activity/tour booking (Flybook, Aspio, Planyo, Firefly Reservations, KoobCamp). They embed booking widgets or iframes on the merchant site and on the server side hold guest profiles for the venue. Typical data captured: name, email, phone, postal/delivery address, party size, dietary or accessibility notes, arrival/departure times, room/table preferences, special-occasion flags and frequently payment cards or BNPL hand-offs. Hotel/PMS deployments additionally collect passport/ID numbers, nationality, date of birth and stay history (registration-form/police-report obligations in EU/UK/CN/AE). Channel managers replicate guest data to OTAs (Booking.com, Expedia, Airbnb), making cross-border transfer the default. Squarely in GDPR/CCPA scope and PCI-DSS scope wherever cards are stored or proxied.
93.name: Reservations & delivery
93.summary_privacy: High PII concentration: contact data, addresses, dietary/health hints, travel history and — for hotels — passport/ID, nationality and DOB collected for registration / police-report obligations. Under GDPR the venue is controller, platform processor; DPA, retention, special-category basis and DSAR required. Tracker moderate on tier-1 delivery (Lieferando heavy, Flipdish/DISH/Guesty moderate), light on long-tail PMS. Channel managers replicate to OTAs — cross-border transfer structural.
93.summary_security: Booking platforms aggregate guest profiles, contact details and often stored cards — hospitality is among the most-breached sectors (Marriott 2018/2020, MGM 2019/2023, IHG 2022, Sabre 2017). Widgets and channel-manager APIs sit in multi-tenant flows; one compromise leaks many venues. PCI-DSS applies where cards are stored/proxied; tokenisation varies between tier-1 PMS (Cloudbeds, Guesty, Eviivo) and long-tail apps. Staff-dashboard auth and OTA-replication endpoints are weak points.

94.description: Referral marketing platforms (Mention Me, Extole, Friendbuy, ReferralCandy, Buyapowa, Talkable, SaaSquatch, Ambassador, Viral Loops, Aklamio, Flocktory, Lootly, EarlyParrot, SparkLoop, Coopt, Refericon, Tapfiliate, eBay Partner Network, Rise.ai) automate refer-a-friend and advocacy programs: they generate per-user share links/codes, attribute referrals across devices via cookies, fingerprints and email matching, distribute rewards and integrate with email service providers, e-commerce platforms and CDPs. They sit on the merchant site as a JS tag and operate a server-side attribution engine that stores both the referrer's and the referee's identifiers (name, email, sometimes social handle, IP, user-agent, purchase value). Because the friend has not transacted with the merchant yet, processing the referee's data is the most contested area legally under GDPR and ePrivacy — France's CNIL, the UK ICO and Spain's AEPD have all flagged refer-a-friend tooling for processing non-customer data without lawful basis, and the ICO's PECR guidance treats unconsented referee email as unlawful direct marketing. CloudSponge, which imports address books from Gmail/Yahoo/Outlook via OAuth, is an especially sensitive sub-case.
94.name: Referral marketing
94.summary_privacy: High privacy risk: cross-device attribution via cookies, fingerprints, email hashes; referee data collected before any contractual relationship — a GDPR/ePrivacy hot spot. CNIL, ICO and AEPD have held unconsented referee processing has no lawful basis; ICO treats unconsented referral email as a PECR breach. Tracker moderate (Friendbuy, Mention Me, Extole, Talkable, ReferralCandy). Referrer -> platform -> ESP -> ad-tech flow makes sharing structural; CCPA disclosures required.
94.summary_security: Moderate surface: small tags but they sit on conversion-critical pages and enable referral fraud, self-referrals, coupon stuffing and email harvesting. CloudSponge address-book imports require OAuth access to the user's mailbox — an attractive phishing vector. Server-side attribution endpoints handle email lists and reward payouts, so ATO or API leakage can spill many merchants' customer data at once. SOC 2 coverage uneven across the long tail (Aklamio, Flocktory, Refericon, Coopt, EarlyParrot).

95.description: Digital asset management (DAM) platforms store, catalogue, transform and distribute brand creative — images, video, 3D, PDFs, brand guidelines, product copy and metadata — for marketing, e-commerce and editorial teams. Examples span enterprise DAMs (Aprimo, Censhare, Canto, Celum, Digizuite, THRON, CoreMedia Content Cloud, Picturepark, MediaValet, Widen, Bynder, Brandfolder, Frontify), PIM/DAM hybrids (Bluestone PIM, Salsify, Plytix), brand-portal tools (Frontify, Corebook), media-management for verticals (Aryeo for real-estate, PhotoShelter for Brands, GraphicsFlow for print, Threekit for 3D, Zmags Creator), image CDNs (Cloudinary, Imgix, Cloudimage), open-source/archival (CollectiveAccess, Mirador, Nextcloud, Keepeek, Archeevo) and file-pipeline services (Transloadit). On the public site DAMs typically appear only as a CDN that serves images/videos; the sensitive surface is the back-office portal where authenticated users upload, tag, approve, license and distribute assets. Tier-1 DAMs hold SOC 2 II + ISO 27001/27018. Some hold pre-release/embargoed material, personally-identifying creative (talent shoots, model releases, customer testimonials), EXIF/IPTC geolocation metadata, and contractual licensing data.
95.name: Digital asset management
95.summary_privacy: Privacy risk is moderate, not high: most assets are corporate creative, not consumer PII. Exposure rises when assets show identifiable people (talent, employees, customer testimonials) — GDPR image-consent / model-release applies — or when metadata holds contributor names, licensing terms or EXIF/IPTC geolocation. Public delivery via DAM CDNs (Cloudinary, Imgix, Cloudimage) rarely sets identifying cookies, so tracker density is light/none across the sample.
