95.summary_security: Primary risks: IP/asset leakage, unauthorised access to embargoed creative, ransomware on the asset store, abuse of public-share links and supply-chain compromise of image-processing pipelines (Transloadit, Cloudinary) decoding untrusted media (ImageMagick/libvips CVE history is long). Public CDN delivery is low-risk; the back-office is the soft target — credential stuffing, weak SSO/MFA, over-broad share links. Tier-1 DAMs (Aprimo, Frontify, Bynder, MediaValet, Widen) hold SOC 2 II + ISO 27001.

96.description: Content curation covers tools that aggregate, filter and re-display third-party or user-generated content (UGC) on a brand's website — social-feed walls and shoppable galleries (Juicer, Tagboard, Tagembed, Foursixty, Covet.pics, Photoslurp, Olapic, Miappi, StoryStream, Postano, FeedMagnet, Cevoid, Bazaarvoice Curation, Pixlee TurnTo, Nosto Visual UGC, Emplifi UGC), content-discovery and editorial-curation tools (Scoop.it, ContentGems, Curated, Sniply, Contently, ContentStudio, Frase), publisher exchanges (CPEx, ePublishing), AI content-generation pipelines (ContentBot, Contents, Klangoo, Rock Content) and UGC commerce widgets (Letro, SnapSea, UGC Creative). They pull posts and media from Instagram, TikTok, X, Facebook and customer-review systems via API, store them server-side, and embed them as widgets/iframes on the merchant site. Because the embeds reference social CDNs and tracking scripts, and because curated posts contain personal data of the original posters (sometimes minors), the category pulls users into ePrivacy/cookie scope, UGC rights/consent obligations, and — in the EU since Feb 2024 — the Digital Services Act (Art 14/16 notice-and-takedown, Art 28 minors protection where applicable).
96.name: Content curation
96.summary_privacy: UGC contains personal data of the original poster (handle, photo, location, sometimes minors); GDPR/CCPA require lawful basis, documented rights-management and a takedown path. EU Digital Services Act (Feb 2024) adds Art 14/16 notice-and-action duties. Tier-1 (Bazaarvoice, Pixlee, Emplifi, Nosto, Flockler, CrowdRiff) provides consent and audit trails; long tail (Tagboard, Foursixty, Tagembed) often does not. Embeds load tracking from Instagram/TikTok/Facebook, triggering ePrivacy consent.
96.summary_security: Widgets are third-party JS embedded on commerce pages — usual supply-chain risk (Magecart-style tampering, malicious update). They render user-uploaded media, adding image/SVG/HTML-injection and stored-XSS risk where sanitisation is weak. Link-shorteners like Sniply rewrite outbound traffic and are an explicit phishing/abuse vector. Few long-tail vendors publish SOC 2; assess CSP scope, iframe sandboxing and UGC moderation pipelines.

97.description: Customer Data Platform (CDP) is the privacy boss-tier of the marketing stack. CDPs (Segment/Twilio, Tealium AudienceStream, mParticle, BlueConic, Adobe Experience Platform Identity Service, Acquia/AgilOne, Lytics, Hull, Simon, Ometria, Lexer, Custobar, Leadspace, SALESmanago, Squeezely, Spotler Activate, Datatrics, FirstHive, Antsomi CDP 365, Bread & Butter, Fueled, Gravito, Enkod, Aptania, Registria, CleverData, Cooladata, Fullpath, PriceSpider, June, Omeda, Optimove, Insider, Exponea, Emarsys, Listrak, Netcore Cloud, Salesforce Audience Studio, Sitecore Engagement Cloud, Treasure Data, Zeotap, Stibo, Rudderstack, Klaviyo Data Platform) ingest first- and third-party event streams from web, app, CRM, POS, email and ad platforms, resolve them to a unified person via identity-graph stitching (deterministic IDs + probabilistic matching on device/IP/email-hash), build segments and push audiences back out to dozens of downstream destinations (Meta, Google Ads, TikTok, email, SMS, push, ad-tech). The result is a centralised, high-fidelity profile of an identified consumer containing behavioural history, contact details, consent state, predicted-value scores and often sensitive attributes inferred via ML. Every major data-protection regime applies; CPRA explicitly treats CDP-to-ad-destination flows as "sharing for cross-context behavioural advertising".
97.name: Customer data platform
97.summary_privacy: Worst-case privacy profile in the marketing stack: persistent identity stitching, deterministic + probabilistic matching, profile enrichment and audience export to many ad destinations. Routinely "selling/sharing" under CCPA/CPRA; lawful basis + granular consent required under GDPR. Tracker moderate-to-heavy (Emarsys, SALESmanago, Listrak, Salesforce Audience Studio, Tealium heavy). HIPAA rare — only Tealium private-cloud is HIPAA-suitable; Segment, mParticle, BlueConic refuse PHI.
97.summary_security: CDPs centralise the customer-data lake — one compromise (token leak, misconfigured destination, malicious reverse-ETL connector, insider abuse) exposes profiles across every channel. Twilio's 2022 Segment-adjacent phishing is a precedent. Client SDKs (Segment analytics.js, Tealium utag.js, mParticle, BlueConic) are first-party-served with deep DOM access — Magecart targets. Server APIs handle identity-resolution and webhook fan-out, amplifying IAM mistakes. Tier-1 hold SOC 2 II + ISO 27001.

98.description: Cart abandonment tools (Recart, Recapture, Barilliance, OptiMonk, Justuno, CartStack, CareCart, Jilt, BiteSpeed) detect when shoppers add items to a cart but leave without completing checkout, then attempt to recover the sale through email reminders, SMS, web push notifications, WhatsApp/Messenger, retargeting ads, and exit-intent popups. They typically rely on client-side tracking scripts, browser fingerprinting, cookie/local-storage identifiers, and integrations with email/ESP platforms (Klaviyo, Mailchimp), ad networks (Meta, Google), and ecommerce platforms (Shopify, WooCommerce, Magento). Because they need to associate an anonymous cart with a contactable identity (email, phone, push token), they aggressively capture and share PII with third-party messaging and advertising endpoints.
98.name: Cart abandonment
98.summary_privacy: Critical privacy impact: the model is to re-identify abandoning visitors and forward email/phone/cart contents to ESPs, SMS, push, Messenger/WhatsApp and ad-retargeting endpoints. Persistent cookies, local-storage IDs and fingerprinting are standard. ePrivacy/GDPR consent is commonly captured post-hoc, falling short of EDPB guidance, and CCPA "sale/share" obligations are routinely triggered by Meta/Google retargeting pixels. Global Privacy Control and opt-out signals are inconsistently honoured.
98.summary_security: Moderate-to-high attack surface: third-party JS on cart/checkout plus webhook/API integrations with store, ESPs, ad networks and push/SMS gateways. Risks: Magecart supply-chain leakage of cart+PII, insecure session/cart-token linking, CSRF/replay in recovery URLs, weak rate limiting on identification endpoints. Mostly small-SaaS vendors with variable SOC2 maturity. Shopify Web Pixels / Checkout Extensibility sandboxes mitigate but do not remove exposure for legacy installs.

99.description: Shipping carriers (DHL, UPS, FedEx, Hermes, DPD, SEUR, MRW, Chronopost, Budbee, Yodel, Österreichische Post, CTT, Trunkrs, Relais Colis, Track123 and regional postal operators) handle parcel pickup, transit, and last-mile delivery. From a web-tech perspective the category covers carrier APIs (label generation, rate quoting, tracking), tracking widgets embedded in merchant order pages, and customer-facing tracking portals. They process full shipping addresses, recipient names, phone numbers, email addresses, and order metadata, and often share that data across logistics partners, customs authorities, and notification providers (SMS/email).
99.name: Shipping carriers
99.summary_privacy: High PII: full postal addresses, name, phone, email are unavoidable; metadata is shared with sub-carriers, customs/border authorities and SMS/email gateways. International transfers (SCCs/UK IDTA), GDPR Art. 28 terms and onward-transfer disclosure are central. Label/API tracker density is light, but consumer tracking portals (DHL/UPS/FedEx) load analytics/ad tags, triggering cookie-law/ePrivacy consent and CCPA opt-out. Tracking-number enumeration and smishing reuse are recurring risks.
99.summary_security: Carriers run sprawling APIs (rating, labels, tracking), public tracking portals and merchant webhooks. Abuse: smishing impersonating tracking notices (FTC/postal advisories 2023-24), enumerable tracking-number lookups leaking recipient PII, OAuth/API-key leakage from store integrations, ATO of shipper dashboards. Majors (DHL/UPS/FedEx/USPS/Royal Mail) hold SOC2/ISO 27001; regional postal/last-mile are uneven. Royal Mail LockBit (Jan 2023) shows network-layer ransomware exposure.

100.description: Shopify apps are third-party extensions distributed through the Shopify App Store that add functionality to merchant stores: upsells, reviews, page builders, custom fields, loyalty, returns, subscriptions, push, analytics, and more. Apps inject script tags (via ScriptTag or the Theme App Extension framework), embed Liquid snippets, register webhooks, and call the Shopify Admin/Storefront GraphQL APIs under broad OAuth scopes that frequently include read_customers, read_orders, and write_orders. Many also load their own remote JavaScript on storefront and checkout pages.
100.name: Shopify apps
100.summary_privacy: Apps routinely receive customer name, email, shipping address, order history and behavioural events, often forwarded to their own analytics, marketing and ad partners under inconsistent DPAs. Sub-processor disclosure, international transfers and CCPA "sale/share" classification vary widely. CMP integration and GPC honouring are inconsistent; many apps load Meta/TikTok/Google pixels on storefront pages bypassing the merchant CMP. Shopify Customer Privacy API adoption is improving.
100.summary_security: Very high attack surface, like WordPress plugins. Each app injects third-party JS (ScriptTag, Theme App Extension or, on checkout, Web Pixels/Checkout UI Extensions) plus OAuth with broad scopes (read_customers, read_orders, write_orders). Risks: Magecart/skimmers via compromised CDNs (Sansec 2020-24), vulnerable apps (XSS/IDOR/SSRF) leaking PII at scale, 2024 Polyfill.io attack, abandoned apps with live tokens, scope creep. BFS review and Web Pixels/Checkout sandboxes help, not remove.

101.description: Recruitment and staffing platforms (Greenhouse, Lever, Workable, JobAdder, Eploy, Jobylon, Comeet, Vincere, ApplicantStack, Hireology, Radancy, Ellucian CRM Recruit, HrFlow.ai, PCRecruiter, OTYS, onlyfy) are applicant tracking systems (ATS), career-site builders, and recruitment CRMs. They ingest highly sensitive applicant data: full names, contact details, work history, resumes/CVs, cover letters, references, salary expectations, and increasingly demographic, EEO/DEI, and right-to-work data, plus assessment and interview recordings. Many integrate AI screening/scoring, video interviewing, background-check vendors, and HRIS systems, and embed tracking pixels on public job pages.
101.name: Recruitment & staffing
101.summary_privacy: Among the most privacy-sensitive web categories. Resumes carry identity, location, education, work history, health hints and protected-class signals. GDPR Art. 9 special-category data (health, ethnicity via DEI forms) and lawful-basis are recurring issues. CCPA HR exemption expired 1 Jan 2023, putting applicant data fully in scope. Retention of unsuccessful applicants, automated rejection and US-vendor transfers (Schrems II / EU-US DPF) are persistent gaps. FERPA covers campus-recruiting data.
101.summary_security: High-value targets holding resumes for entire applicant pools. Incidents: exposed S3 buckets of CVs, broken access on candidate portals, IDOR in ATS APIs, downstream exposure from BG-check vendors (MOVEit/PBI 2023). BG-check and ID-verification extend the trust chain into government databases. Career-site widget injection is a recurring XSS surface. Algorithmic screening and one-way video interviewing trigger EU AI Act Annex III high-risk status and NYC AEDT Local Law 144 annual bias audits.

102.description: Returns management platforms (Loop Returns, Narvar, Happy Returns, AfterShip Returns Center, Returnly, ReturnGO, Return Prime, Seko OmniReturns, Reshop, Refundid, Seel, ParcelPanel, ShippyPro) provide branded return portals, RMA workflows, label generation, exchange/store-credit logic, and integrations with carriers and ecommerce platforms. They handle customer identity, order history, return reasons (sometimes including health/sizing context), refund routing, and in some cases instant-refund underwriting. Most operate as SaaS with embedded JS widgets on merchant storefronts and webhook integrations into Shopify/Magento/BigCommerce.
102.name: Returns
102.summary_privacy: Returns combine identity, address, order content and free-text return-reason fields that often carry sensitive context (health, sizing, complaints). Data is shared with carriers, the merchant, fraud-scoring (Riskified/Signifyd) and recommerce partners (Reshop, Recurate). GDPR data-minimisation and retention on reason text are weak. Branded portals inherit merchant analytics/CMP tags; tracker density is light on the core, heavier on embedding pages. CCPA "sale/share" can fire post-confirmation.
102.summary_security: Moderate attack surface. Return portals expose order-number+email lookups enabling enumeration; weak rate limits, predictable RMA tokens and tokenless return links are recurring bugs. Carrier-label generation handles UPS/USPS/DHL/FedEx API keys; instant-refund (Seel, Refundid, Reshop) adds payment-routing and tokenised cards. Webhooks into Shopify/Magento/BigCommerce inherit OAuth exposure. Loop, Narvar, Happy Returns and AfterShip hold SOC2 Type II; long-tail Shopify-app vendors are uneven.

103.description: Livestreaming platforms and SDKs (Cloudflare Stream, Kaltura, Loom, Vonage Video API, Dyte, Bambuser, Firework, Livescale, BigMarker, MediaPlatform, Klarna Virtual Shopping, Trovo, EasyWebinar, Conviva, Apizee) ingest, transcode, distribute, and analyse live or near-live video over HLS/DASH/WebRTC. Use cases include broadcast streaming, webinars, live-shopping, video customer service, and embedded player widgets. They collect device, network, geolocation, viewer identity, chat content, and detailed quality-of-experience telemetry; live-commerce variants additionally integrate cart and payment flows.
103.name: Livestreaming
103.summary_privacy: Players and analytics SDKs set persistent IDs and report telemetry to the vendor and to ad/analytics partners (Conviva, Google, Meta). Chat and engagement may carry identity. Live-shopping pulls in payment and identity. Kid audiences (gaming/creator, education) trigger COPPA and FTC 2024 amendments; very-large platforms also face EU DSA/AVMSD. Cookie-law/ePrivacy and CCPA opt-out signals on embedded players are inconsistently honoured; recording retention and transcript-AI add secondary risk.
103.summary_security: Mixed attack surface: WebRTC/SFU services expose signalling endpoints and TURN creds; HLS/DASH/LL-HLS players are large JS bundles loaded across many origins. Risks: stream-key leakage enabling unauthorised broadcasts, DRM/token flaws, chat-XSS, recording-storage misconfig (open S3/GCS), credential-stuffing of creator dashboards. Cloudflare Stream, Kaltura, AWS IVS and Mux hold SOC2/ISO 27001; live-commerce and smaller WebRTC vendors are uneven. 2024 Snowflake compromises hit adjacent media.

104.description: Ticket booking and event registration platforms (Etix, Ticketure, Tix, Tickera, SquadUP, PromoTix, HoldMyTicket, Race Roster, Haku, Droplabs, Ventrata, Beyonk, Saffire, Bileto, Cooltix, Asksuite, ClickBus, KKTIX, Invitario, Gevme, HelixPay, Planning Pod, Fatsoma, Starboard Suite) handle event discovery, seat/inventory selection, attendee registration, payment, and ticket delivery for concerts, tours, attractions, transport, and conferences. They process buyer and attendee identity, contact details, payment card data (usually via tokenised PSPs), and increasingly ID-verification, age-gating, and vaccination/health data, plus location and behavioural data for fraud and dynamic pricing.
104.name: Ticket booking
104.summary_privacy: High PII concentration: legal name, contact, payment, sometimes gov-ID and post-COVID health data, plus behavioural and location data used for dynamic pricing, demand modelling and re-marketing. Data is shared with promoters, venues, processors, fraud vendors, sponsor/list-rental and ad-retargeting; primary-to-secondary resale complicates consent. GDPR lawful-basis and retention are central; CCPA "sale/share" fires from Meta/Google pixels on events. COPPA for youth; EU DSA for large primaries.
104.summary_security: Critical. Public checkout, huge attendee databases and QR/barcode issuance are heavily targeted by credential-stuffing, scalper bots (OWASP OAT-005/012), card-testing and ATO. Defining incident: Ticketmaster/Snowflake breach (May 2024) exposing ~560M records via a non-MFA Snowflake tenant; recurring breaches at See Tickets, AXS partners and smaller vendors. PCI-DSS scope is unavoidable; hosted PSP fields (Stripe, Adyen) reduce not remove it. WAF, MFA and signed rotating tickets are core.

105.description: Augmented reality (AR) technologies overlay computer-generated content onto live camera feeds or environmental scans, enabling virtual try-on (eyewear, cosmetics, apparel, jewellery), product visualisation, 3D model viewing, and immersive ecommerce experiences. Vendors range from face-tracking SDKs (DeepAR, ModiFace, Perfect Corp, YouCam) and virtual fitting solutions (Fittingbox, Luna, Auglio, Tangiblee, mirrAR) to 3D viewer/configurator platforms (Threekit, Vectary, Cylindo, <model-viewer>, A-Frame). They typically require camera access, may capture facial geometry, body measurements, depth/spatial data, and run as client-side JavaScript/WebGL or native SDKs embedded in retailer storefronts and mobile apps.
105.name: Augmented reality
105.summary_privacy: AR/virtual try-on captures facial geometry, body measurements, depth maps, gaze and live video — biometric special-category data under GDPR Art. 9, BIPA, Texas CUBI, WA MHMDA and CCPA/CPRA sensitive-PI. Active BIPA class actions against Estee Lauder, Louis Vuitton, Warby Parker, Sephora and L'Oreal show real exposure. Many SDKs phone home, blend images into model-training pipelines and rely on third-party CDNs. Opt-in, on-device processing, retention caps and training opt-out are required.
105.summary_security: AR SDKs run client-side with broad camera/sensor permissions and load heavy third-party JS/WebGL bundles; supply-chain compromise of the vendor script yields webcam hijack, MediaStream theft and facial-geometry exfiltration. Native SDKs ship as opaque binaries, rarely audited, and bundle telemetry to vendor CDNs. WebXR / USDZ / model-loader parsers see a steady CVE cadence. Attack surface is moderate but blast radius (live camera, depth sensor, DOM injection) is severe.

106.description: Cross-border ecommerce platforms enable retailers to sell internationally by handling localised storefronts, multi-currency pricing, duty/tax calculation (DDP), VAT/IOSS registration, customs documentation, international shipping orchestration, fraud screening and global payment acceptance. Vendors include merchant-of-record providers (ESW, Global-e, Zonos, Borderfree, Passport), localisation specialists (Glopal, Localised), payments-led players (Flow, Reach) and region-specific gateways (Azoya for China, WorldShopping for Japan). They sit between the merchant store and the consumer, handling identity, address, payment and tax-ID data across many legal jurisdictions.
106.name: Cross border ecommerce
106.summary_privacy: Move PII across jurisdictions under GDPR, UK GDPR, FADP, CCPA/CPRA, LGPD, PIPL and post-Schrems II SCC + transfer-impact-assessment rules. PIPL's 2024 standard contract, India's DPDP, China/Russia data-localisation and IOSS/OSS VAT reporting force lawful but extensive sharing with carriers, customs, tax authorities and banks. Multi-jurisdiction consent (ePrivacy, GPC), DSAR and long tax retention are non-trivial. Customer ID-doc collection for customs is common and frequently mishandled.
106.summary_security: Merchant-of-record platforms aggregate cardholder data, fraud scores, addresses, tax IDs and customs declarations across thousands of merchants — PCI-DSS L1 + SOC 2 high-value targets demanding tokenisation. MoR providers inject checkout JS and 3DS redirects, widening supply-chain attack surface (Magecart e-skimming on MoR scripts is documented). Cross-border traffic and varied IP geos complicate WAF tuning. A breach can leak millions of orders' PAN, address and ID-doc data.

107.description: Fulfilment technologies handle the post-checkout supply-chain workflow: warehouse management (WMS), order routing, multi-carrier shipping label generation, inventory sync, returns processing and consumer-facing shipment tracking. Examples include WMS/order platforms (VeraCore, MapMyChannel, Bobgo, Enviopack), branded tracking & shipping protection (AfterShip, Malomo, Route, Extend), 3PL operators (Bleckmann, Deliverr, Planzer) and large logistics platforms (Descartes, ShopRunner). They process customer name, shipping address, phone, order contents and sometimes signed delivery proofs, integrating via API/webhooks with Shopify, Magento, NetSuite and carrier networks.
107.name: Fulfilment
107.summary_privacy: Process high-volume shipping PII (name, address, phone, sometimes passport/national-ID for customs, GPS-tagged POD photos, signature capture) under GDPR, CCPA/CPRA and FADP; carriers and customs brokers receive onward transfers. Branded-tracking pages embed retargeting pixels requiring ePrivacy consent. Returns capture reason codes and damage photos. Retention is long (tax, customs — 7-10 years); breach-notification duties hit both merchant-controller and 3PL-processor.
107.summary_security: 3PL systems are a supply-chain choke point: millions of address/order pairs, carrier API keys, WMS access, EDI/ERP links. Third-party breaches drive ~30% of incidents (Verizon DBIR 2024); webhook/EDI integrations are prime targets for credential abuse and ransomware. VeraCore CVE-2024-57968 + CVE-2025-25181 were chained by XE Group 2024-25 for webshells. Branded-tracking pixels (AfterShip, Route, Malomo) inject post-purchase JS, adding e-skimming risk.

108.description: Ecommerce frontends are the storefront rendering layer of an online shop — themes, headless/PWA frameworks and frontend-as-a-service platforms that talk to a backend commerce engine (Magento, Shopify, BigCommerce, commercetools). Includes open-source themes/frameworks (Hyva, Breeze, ScandiPWA, Vue Storefront, PWA Studio, Front-Commerce, E-Com Plus), commercial FE platforms (Shogun Frontend, Makaira, Deco.cx, Platter) and product-customisers/UX add-ons (Aiden, Kickflip, Argento). They run primarily in the browser, render checkout pages, and load many third-party scripts (payment, analytics, tag managers, chat).
108.name: Ecommerce frontends
108.summary_privacy: Storefronts collect identifiers, browsing behaviour, cart contents and checkout PII (name, email, address, phone) under GDPR, ePrivacy, CCPA/CPRA, LGPD. Heavy third-party scripts (analytics, ads, A/B, personalisation, reviews) make IAB TCF / GPC consent management load-bearing. CDPs profile users and fan data to marketing clouds; ad pixels (Meta, TikTok, Google) leak email hashes and order values. Card entry delegates to PCI iframes but every script stays in PCI 6.4.3 scope.
108.summary_security: Frontends render the checkout — the prime Magecart / e-skimming target, hitting ~11k stores in 2024 (3x YoY per Sansec/Trustwave). CosmicSting (CVE-2024-34102) compromised ~75% of Adobe Commerce / Magento stores; CVE-2024-20720 and 2024 trojanised Magento extensions (21 vendors) followed. Headless PWAs (Hyva, Vue Storefront, PWA Studio) widen API surface; themes inherit CMS CVEs. Storefronts embed 10-30 scripts. PCI v4.0.1 6.4.3 + 11.6.1 require script inventory and tamper detection.

109.description: Domain parking refers to the practice of monetising unused or registered-but-undeveloped domain names by serving them through a parking page — typically an auto-generated lander filled with pay-per-click (PPC) advertising, related-keyword links, "Buy this domain" forms, or affiliate redirects. Operators include registrar-attached parking services (GoDaddy CashParking, Arsys, Verisign-adjacent ecosystems), dedicated parking networks (Sedo, Bodis, Team Internet/Skenzo, Above.com) and free-subdomain offerings (JS.org). Parked pages are static or thin client pages and frequently use bidstream ad networks, server-side fingerprinting and conditional redirects (cloaking).
109.name: Domain parking
109.summary_privacy: Parking pages monetise traffic with dense ad-tech: RTB bidstream, canvas/audio fingerprinting, geo-IP profiling, cookie syncing across DSPs/SSPs and persistent IDs (UID2, RampID). They typically lack a privacy policy, fail GDPR/ePrivacy consent and pass visitor signals to unknown third parties with no DPA. Fingerprint-driven conditional redirects decide whether to show ads, scams or a placeholder, making DSAR and erasure effectively impossible. CCPA "sale/share" disclosures are absent.
109.summary_security: Infoblox/Krebs (Dec 2025): over 90% of parked-domain visits now lead to malicious content — scams, drive-by malware, fake CAPTCHA / ClickFix, tech-support fraud, phishing kits — a tenfold rise since 2014. "Direct search" monetisation replaced AdSense for Domains after Google opted advertisers out, removing the last brand-safety controls. Landers cloak by IP, ASN, UA, cookie and TLS-fingerprint. Typosquats impersonate banks and SaaS; chains terminate in browser-RCE or stealer payloads.

110.description: Form builders are no-code/low-code platforms that let non-developers create web forms, surveys, quizzes, multi-step funnels, lead-capture pop-ups and registration flows that are embedded on third-party sites or hosted on the vendor's domain. Submissions are stored in the vendor's cloud and forwarded to email, CRMs and webhooks. Examples span SaaS giants and embedded niche tools (Typeform-like Heyflow, Formsite, Marquiz, Formaloo, Reform, Formless, NativeForms), backend-only services (Basin, Slapform), WordPress/Shopify plugins (Quform, Globo, HulkApps, Pabbly, Funnelforms) and HIPAA-specific (FormDr).
110.name: Form builders
110.summary_privacy: Forms collect sensitive PII (name, email, phone, address, sometimes health, financial, government-ID, biometric, minors' data) triggering GDPR, CCPA/CPRA, HIPAA, COPPA and PCI. Vendors are processors requiring DPAs/BAAs/SCCs; many embed analytics, hCaptcha/reCAPTCHA, pixels and CRM webhooks fanning submissions to dozens of third parties. Consent, lawful basis, retention and DSAR are routinely missed by SMB users. Auto-save and partial submissions persist data respondents never meant to send.
110.summary_security: Form builders run client-side (JS/iframes) and store free-text on vendor servers — targets for credential phishing, IDOR on submission URLs, SSRF via webhooks and supply-chain JS injection. Notable: 2018 Typeform breach (~16k customers), Gravity Forms July 2025 supply-chain compromise, Ninja Forms CVE-2023-37979/CVE-2024-13683, Contact Form 7 CVE-2020-35489. Uploads bypass at-rest encryption; webhook secrets leak in public repos. PCI 6.4.3 and HIPAA BAA scope creep with payment/health fields.

111.description: Fundraising & donations platforms enable charities, nonprofits, educational institutions, political campaigns and crowdfunding causes to accept online contributions. They provide donation forms, recurring-giving, peer-to-peer pages, text-to-give, mobile bidding/auctions, donor CRMs and receipt/tax-letter generation. Examples include Donorbox, GiveWP, Classy, Qgiv, Kindful, Raisely, GiveSmart, OneCause, FundRazr, iRaiser, GiveCampus, RaiseDonors, Vanco, Click & Pledge, Frontstream, Neon CRM and microlender Kiva. They embed widgets/iframes on charity sites or host donation pages on the vendor domain, integrating with Stripe, Braintree, PayPal and ACH processors.
111.name: Fundraising & donations
111.summary_privacy: Process sensitive donor records — name, address, email, phone, payment instrument, gift amount, schedule, plus religious, political, health-cause or alumni affiliation often qualifying as GDPR Art. 9 / CCPA sensitive-PI. Tax-receipt rules drive 7+ year retention (IRS 8283, Gift Aid). Donor CRMs sync to email, ad-audiences and wealth-screening append (DonorSearch, WealthEngine, iWave), widening disclosure. Anonymous-giving, lawful basis, FERPA on alumni data and DPAs with sub-processors required.
111.summary_security: Donation platforms touch card data, ACH/IBAN, recurring tokens and donor identity — direct fraud/skimming targets. Carding on donation forms is common (Donorbox). Critical vulns: GiveWP CVE-2024-5932 (unauth RCE via PHP object injection, 100k+ sites, Wordfence Aug 2024) + CVE-2021-24917 (SQLi); Blackbaud 2020 ransomware exposed ~6M records, $49.5M state-AG settlement. Most are PCI L1 via Stripe iframes, but merchants miss v4.0.1 6.4.3 script-inventory. Political platforms face FEC rules.
